responseSet.title: Laplanche in the Consulting Room: An Emerging Paradigm for Psychoanalytic Therapeutic Action
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Laplanche in the Consulting Room: An Emerging Paradigm for Psychoanalytic Therapeutic Action

Sam Guzzardi LCSW

Sam Guzzardi

32 Union Square East, Suite, New York, NY 1106, USA
sgpsychotherapy@gmail.com

Sam Guzzardi   New York City-based psychoanalyst trained at the Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity, IPSS. His written work on gender and sexuality in psychoanalysis has won numerous awards, including the Ralph Roughton Award of the American Psychoanalytic Association and, most recently, the 2025 Tiresias Award of the International Psychoanalytical Association. He is on the faculty of the National Institute of the Psychotherapies (NIP) and IPSS, and is an associate editor at Psychoanalysis, Self, and Context and at Psychoanalytic Inquiry. His clinical work focuses on questions of gender, trauma, development, sexuality, and loss.

This paper explores the clinical possibilities in Jean Laplanche’s metapsychology and theory of psychoanalytic action. It examines Laplanche’s conceptualizations of the drive, the ego, binding and unbinding, and translation and detranslation, and considers how they may be of use in thinking about transformation in psychoanalytic treatment. In particular, the author considers his experiences with two patients, reflecting on how his affinity for Laplanche’s theory may have had an effect on the trajectory of those experiences. Concepts such as the tub, tangentializing the concerns of the ego, and unbinding as gateway to new configurations of self are elucidated in the context of the two treatments. The author resists notions of orthodoxy and purity as clinical dicta, and instead argues for the co-existence of psychotherapeutic and psychoanalytic praxis, positing that a pursuit of Laplanchean psychoanalytic experience does not negate other more “bound” approaches but rather, following Laplanche, tangentializes them.

responseSet.title: Grief and Loss, Hopes and Desires
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Grief and Loss, Hopes and Desires

Chanda D. Griffin LCSW

Chanda D. Griffin

New York, New York, USA

Chanda D. Griffin  

Immediately after the inauguration, a deluge of NYT alerts begins to disrupt my sessions. I can’t seem to silence the beeps, alarms, and dings coming from all my electronic devices. Not only are my “contained” sessions interrupted, but my “grounded” and focused state has been upended as well. What’s the point of titrating the amount of news coverage I consume, if I’m reminded of Trumps’ executive orders every 10 minutes! There is a bigger concern, or disruption to my peace bubble—my patients, who embody most of the marginalized identities that are and will be harmfully impacted and under attack: Black, POC, LGBTQIA+. Many of these folx have dedicated their lives to DEI work (before it was called DEI) and are frantically trying to save their programs; many are in fear of losing their jobs. Patients with trans children are both angry and afraid of recent orders to reverse or un-trans (a pairing of undue and transition), via executive orders, to reject their child’s gender status and revert it to the assigned gender at birth. In one session a patient in despair and hopelessness said, “Trump can do whatever the fuck he wants because we no longer have checks and balances. What does this mean for my black trans child when his gender identity goes unrecognized by government? Canada looks really good right now!” Every fiber in my being wants to say, “That’s not true, there will be someone or something to “check and balance” what seems to be this administration’s mission to turn back the hands of time, but I no longer know anymore and ask myself did/do I ever really know. In 5 years, there have been so many unprecedented and surprising events, I can no longer feel a sense of safety or security in what I often believed was knowable and/or predictable. I can’t think about it. I can’t think.

I do know, I’ve been here before. During the height of Covid and the murder of George Floyd, I presented and wrote about (Griffin, in press) how dysregulated I felt, and how it seemed to constrict my ability to respond more fully to my patients. When I think of Bion’s attack on linking/thinking, whereby both analyst and patient are “paused” or halted by “parasitic” communications that both obstruct and destroy the ability to hold space and contain the affective and somatic reactions in the room, I generally can attribute these phenomena to both of our histories, internalized representations and beliefs that are unique to both of us. Unlike the “containment” in only addressing familial and the here and now of our working relationship, the seeming boundlessness of human depravity and repeated devaluation of Black and Brown bodies has entered the consulting room! The sociopolitical upheaval and continuous eruptions in government have become a source that attacks linking—links to thinking and links to each other when connection is what we so desperately need at this time. I asked the question of what to expect from Bion’s container/contained and efforts to listen without memory or desire when both patient and I are having a trauma response of overwhelm, fear, anxiety, and powerlessness.

responseSet.title: In This Issue, Psychoanalytic Dialogues 35–5
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In This Issue, Psychoanalytic Dialogues 35–5

Stephen Hartman PHD and Lauren Levine PHD

Lauren Levine

Lauren Levine  

Were this issue of Psychoanalytic Dialogues being presented live or as a podcast, one thing would stand out immediately: It speaks to the array of psychoanalytic perspectives on topics as foundational as early experience in the human body and as contemporary as the way social rhythms blend recursively into the story of psychic life in a wealth of accents. Relational psychoanalysis is indeed polyglot. Inspired by Gila Ashtor’s inquiry into the problem of “pseudo self” experience, Tara Chivukula’s linguistic trips across continents and in the consulting room, and Ricardo Lombardi’s continuing investigation of body/mind dynamics, this issue is an aural treat.

Everyone has an accent of some sort. So, we leave it to you to hear the voices of our authors and discussants each in the integrity of their idiomatic manner of speech, in their cultural and theoretical surround, and in the various combinations of accents that manifest in how they sound in dialogue. This issue is not shy. Translation among comparative points of view among diverse speakers from different disciplines, backgrounds, and geographic centers of psychoanalytic thought inevitably prompts a great deal of affect. Dialogues is committed to hearing every author and discussant’s manner of formulating their ideas and to our shared discipline, just as we invite conversations that shake it up! This, to our ear, is the recipe for a healthy, comparative, and pluralistic psychoanalysis.

También notarás que el idioma español se ha convertido en un elemento habitual en nuestras páginas. You’ll also notice that Spanish has become a regular feature in our pages. Thanks to the IARPP, Dialogues has inspired great interest in Latin America, and we have begun to receive wonderful submissions in Spanish. We welcome this. Inviting discussants and authors of Snapshots and Après Coups essays to write in their native tongue has been a boon to our outreach to Latin America where English is not as often the lingua franca that it is in other parts of the world where psychoanalysis flourishes. We have received feedback from Latin American colleagues that they would eagerly welcome Spanish translations of entire panels so that they may more easily engage cutting-edge psychoanalytic writing without delay. To that end, and with the gran habilidad lingüística of our Peruvian executive editor, Marie Saba, we present the groundbreaking panel led by Gila Ashtor in the original English and in Spanish translation. With this experiment, we renew Dialogues’ commitment to what the Russian literary critic Mikhail Bakhtin in 1982 esteemed the dialogic imagination.

responseSet.title: Dissociation: Defense And/Or Deficit in the Making of a Pseudo Self
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dissociation: Defense And/Or Deficit in the Making of a Pseudo Self

Anne Alvarez PHD

Anne Alvarez

London, UK
annealvarez380@gmail.com

Anne Alvarez   Consultant Child and Adolescent Psychotherapist (retired Co-Convener of the Autism Service), Tavistock Clinic, London. She is the author of Live Company and joint editor of Autism and Personality. Being Alive: Building on the Work of Anne Alvarez (ed. J Edwards), was published in 2002. She is also the Author of The Thinking Heart (2012).

This discussion provides a commentary on Gila Ashtor’s important paper, which addresses the difficulty treating and understanding patients with a normotic or “as if” personality who it turns out are considerably dissociated. The patient Ashtor describes does not appear to be severely dissociated yet seems unable – or unwilling – to follow thoughts through to other thoughts or feelings. This discussion questions whether this is always as defensive as Ashtor thinks – thus, whether there is a more depressed/deprived response to an object conceived not as bad or frightening, but inadequate and relatively uninteresting. The consequence may be a limp response to a weakly engaging object.

responseSet.title: Hypnoid States, the Structuralization of Mind, and the Interpersonal/Relational Understanding of Dissociation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hypnoid States, the Structuralization of Mind, and the Interpersonal/Relational Understanding of Dissociation

Donnel B. Stern PHD

Donnel B. Stern

24 East 82nd St., Suite 1B, New York, NY 10028, USA
donnelstern@gmail.com

Donnel B. Stern   Training and Supervising Analyst at the William Alanson White Institute in New York City; and Adjunct Clinical Professor and Clinical Consultant in the NYU Postdoctoral Program of Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. His most recent book is On Coming Into Possession of Oneself: Transformations of the Interpersonal Field (2024). He is in private practice in New York City.

In this article, I discuss Gila Ashtor’s “The Pseudo-Self: Dissociation and Feeling Disorders.” I suggest that Ashtor’s account of the interpersonal/relational theory of dissociation is simplistic and incomplete, and I explain why I see her account in that way. The commentary ends with my description of a basic difference between Ashtor’s account of psychoanalytic interpretation, and therefore clinical practice, and my own.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Babel: Our Accents, Ourselves

Veronica Csillag LCSW

Veronica Csillag

New York, New York, USA
veronica.csillag@gmail.com

Veronica Csillag   Faculty, Training and Supervising Analyst, and former Co-Director, Manhattan Institute for Psychoanalysis; Faculty, National Institute for the Psychotherapies. Associate Editor, Psychoanalytic Dialogues. She is the author of several psychoanalytic papers, published in The American Journal of Psychoanalysis, Psychoanalytic Dialogues and The Psychoanalytic Quarterly among them. Author, editor, From Budapest to Psychoanalysis: Three Portraits and their Analytic Frames, Routledge, 2023. She is in private practice in New York City.

In her thoughtful and beautifully written paper, Tara Chivukula takes on a hitherto somewhat unexplored aspect of psychoanalytic theorizing, accents and their role in social stratification as well as their importance in shaping the transferential matrix. In my discussion I follow up on some of these themes. Immigration, race, class and economic status feature in my contemplations on these matters from a theoretical as well as from a personal perspective. While I make a case for migration and the polyglot nature of culture, I also acknowledge the heartache involved in forever being on the move, leaving friends, family and the landscapes of home behind.

responseSet.title: Standing in Between Accents, Listening for Whispers
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Standing in Between Accents, Listening for Whispers

Marie Saba MA

Marie Saba

Choquehuanca 1495, Apt. 502, San Isidro, Lima, Peru
msabav@gmail.com

Marie Saba   Clinical psychologist and psychoanalyst in private practice in Lima, Peru. She is the Founding President of the Peruvian Chapter of the International Association of Relational Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy and currently serves as Executive Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues: The International Journal of Relational Perspectives. She is also Director of the publishing house Espacio Gradiva and co-editor, with Jean Magagna, of El Niño en Silencio (London: Karnac, 2014).

In this commentary on Tara Chivukula’s paper, Saba follows the resonances Chivukula awakens – listening for the ways accents, more than mere inflections of sound, carry memory, displacement, and longing. Chivukula invites us to consider how accents both expose and conceal, how they can reduce a person to stereotype while also holding the possibility of play, recognition, and creativity. Saba reflects on Chivukula’s call to hear accents in breath, pauses, and hesitations – listening for whispers that escape categorization. In dialogue with her work, Saba asks how psychoanalysis can become a space where the accented voice is not flattened but held, where paradoxes are sustained rather than erased. Standing between accents, Saba explores how difference can generate connection, how sound itself – following Winnicott – may become a transitional object, and how listening attuned to resonance can help sustain the immigrant’s right to go on being. But that time was broken.

[[RunningHead]]

The Pseudo-Self: Dissociation and Feeling Disorders: Reply to Discussions

Gila Ashtor PHD L.P.

Gila Ashtor

239 Central Park West, #1BE, New York, NY 10024, USA
Doctorashtor@gmail.com

Gila Ashtor   Assistant Professor of Clinical Psychoanalysis at Columbia University. She is also on the Faculty at New York University’s Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy, and at IPTAR. She is the author of three books, Homo Psyche: On Queer Theory and Erotophobia (Fordham UP, 2021), which won the MLA’s Alan Bray Award for Best Book and was a finalist for the Lambda Literary Award, Exigent Psychoanalysis: The Interventions of Jean Laplanche (Routledge, 2021), and Aural History (Punctum, 2020). She is currently at work on a book about feeling disorders – phenomena such as gaslighting, empty consent, and toxic relationships – that combines her interdisciplinary expertise. Her primary areas of academic and clinical expertise include dissociative disorders, trauma, and sexuality. She is in private practice in New York City.

Response to Discussants

Something that struck me about each of these remarkable responses is that they all, in one way or another, remark on my “strong” insistence that defense analysis must be a signal component of the treatment protocol for “pseudo-self” disorders. Anne Alvarez, Peter Goldberg, and Donnel Stern are some of my favorite thinkers on this topic, and I was struck by the fact that they all in different ways express some degree of curiosity, perhaps even mystification, about why I come out as strongly against “catalyzing transformations” as I do. Although they were each gracious about this in their own way, when I saw myself through their own eyes, I saw that I was unduly preoccupied with making a point about defense analysis that I no longer easily understood or identified with. To wit, rereading my own paper alongside these thoughtful and engaged responses, I began to wonder what I had against “catalyzing transformations?” I was especially perplexed because I was a firm believer in technical innovations that stretched and expanded the frame to meet patients where they were at, and I didn’t fully recognize myself as someone suddenly advocating for defense analysis or, god forbid, interpretation instead of “catalyzing transformations.”

In what follows, I want to revisit the question of how we treat patients with “pseudo-self” disorders by 1) reanimating the context in which defense analysis and non-interpretative interventions are continually counterposed, and 2) revisiting my clinical example to specify the range of work these conditions require.

Let me start with something that happened in my treatment with Casey. I have already mentioned that she was a talented music teacher in her mid-40s, but now I want to explore her relationship to music in greater depth because I think it tells us something important about the complex interrelationship between dissociation as a self-regulatory mechanism and the mind’s other defensive operations. A significant detail from Casey’s family background is that her mother was an established musician of some renown, as was her maternal grandmother. All these women attended the same musical conservatory and – on the basis of her family’s connections as well as her own nascent interest in music – Casey was invited to matriculate there as well. But when it came time to decide on colleges, Casey chose to go somewhere else. Her choice of a mediocre liberal arts college surprised her mother a bit, but her parents were reluctant to sway her career path and said nothing. At college, Casey dabbled in some music courses, but she was otherwise pretty uninterested and uninspired by her coursework. She did well enough and showed some genuine talent in several music-

donnelstern@gmail.com

Donnel B. Stern   Analista didacta y supervisor en el William Alanson White Institute de Nueva York, y profesor clínico de Psicología y consultor clínico en el Programa de Posdoctorado en Psicoanálisis y Psicoterapia de la Universidad de Nueva York (NYU). Es fundador y editor de la serie de libros en Routledge Psychoanalysis in a New Key, que cuenta con más de 90 títulos publicados. Fue editor en jefe de la revista Contemporary Psychoanalysis. Ha publicado más de 100 artículos y capítulos de libros en psicoanálisis, ha escrito cinco libros y ha editado otros cuatro. Su libro más reciente, publicado en 2024, es On Coming Into Possession of Oneself: Transformations of the Interpersonal Field.

En este artículo comento “El pseudo-self: disociación y trastornos del sentir” de Gila Ashtor. Sostengo que la exposición de Ashtor sobre la teoría interpersonal/relacional de la disociación es simplista e incompleta, y explico por qué la considero de ese modo. El comentario concluye con mi descripción de una diferencia fundamental entre la concepción de Ashtor sobre la interpretación psicoanalítica —y, por tanto, sobre la práctica clínica— y la mía propia.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Pseudo-Self y el Orden Oculto del Desapego

Peter Goldberg PHD

Peter Goldberg

San Francisco, USA
909 Ordway Street, Albany, CA 94706
petergoldberg@restio.org

Peter Goldberg   Analista personal y supervisor en el Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California, codirector del cuerpo docente en el San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis y miembro de la facultad del Wright Institute en Berkeley. Es coautor de Here I’m Alive: The Spirit of Music in Psychoanalysis (Columbia University Press, 2023) y ha escrito y presentado ampliamente sobre una variedad de temas clínicos y teóricos, incluyendo la evolución de la teoría clínica en psicoanálisis; la disociación psicosomática; la experiencia sensorial en el análisis y en la vida cultural; los mecanismos transicionales y la función del encuadre analítico; los estados no representacionales; y el impacto del trauma social en la psicología individual.

La concepción de Gila Ashtor sobre el pseudo-self se explora en relación con su crítica a tres enfoques clínicos existentes sobre la disociación. Se destacan características importantes del pseudo-self, incluyendo su particular capacidad de fusionarse con las formaciones culturales actuales y su correspondiente invisibilidad en la teoría clínica, así como el tipo reflexivo de desapego que sitúa a estos pacientes fuera del alcance habitual de nuestros métodos clínicos tradicionales. La cuestión del abordaje clínico de la disociación – y del fenómeno del pseudo-self en particular – se considera a la luz de la propuesta de Ashtor de que las estructuras defensivas inconscientes deben ser analizadas para tratar los trastornos disociativos del sentir. Se enfatiza un tipo activo de intervención clínica orientada a establecer contacto con el paciente pseudo-normal en el nivel psico-sensorial de la experiencia compartida.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

El Pseudo-Self: Disociación y Trastornos del Sentir: Repuesta a Las Discusiones

Gila Ashtor PHD L.P.

Gila Ashtor

239 Central Park West, #1BE, Nueva York, NY 10024, USA
doctorashtor@gmail.com

Gila Ashtor   Profesora adjunta de Psicoanálisis Clínico en la Universidad de Columbia. También forma parte de la facultad del Programa de Posdoctorado en Psicoanálisis y Psicoterapia de la Universidad de Nueva York y de IPTAR. Es autora de tres libros: Homo Psyche: On Queer Theory and Erotophobia (Fordham UP, 2021), que obtuvo el Premio Alan Bray de la MLA al mejor libro y fue finalista del Lambda Literary Award; Exigent Psychoanalysis: The Interventions of Jean Laplanche (Routledge, 2021) y Aural History (Punctum, 2020). Actualmente trabaja en un libro sobre los trastornos del sentir —fenómenos como el gaslighting, el consentimiento vacío y las relaciones tóxicas— que combina su experiencia interdisciplinaria. Sus principales áreas de especialización académica y clínica incluyen los trastornos disociativos, el trauma y la sexualidad. Ejerce en consulta privada en la ciudad de Nueva York.

Respuesta a los comentaristas

Algo que me llamó la atención de cada una de estas notables respuestas es que todas, de una u otra manera, comentan mi “firme” insistencia en que el análisis de defensas debe ser un componente esencial del protocolo de tratamiento para los trastornos de “pseudo-self.” Anne Alvarez, Peter Goldberg y Donnel Stern son algunos de mis pensadores favoritos sobre este tema, y me sorprendió que todos ellos, de distintas maneras, expresaran cierto grado de curiosidad– quizá incluso desconcierto– acerca de por qué me pronuncio con tanta fuerza en contra de las “transformaciones catalizadoras”.” Aunque cada uno lo señaló de manera elegante a su modo, al verme a través de sus ojos, noté que estaba indebidamente empeñada en remarcar un punto sobre el análisis de defensas que ya no comprendía o con el que ya no me identificaba del todo. Dicho de otro modo, al releer mi propio artículo junto con estas respuestas tan reflexivas y comprometidas, comencé a preguntarme: ¿qué tenía yo en contra de las “transformaciones catalizadoras?” Me resultaba especialmente desconcertante porque siempre he sido una firme defensora de las innovaciones técnicas que amplían y flexibilizan el encuadre para encontrar a los pacientes allí de se encuentran, y no me reconocía del todo como alguien que de pronto aboga por el análisis de defensas o, dios no lo quiera, por la interpretación, en lugar de las “transformaciones catalizadoras.”

En lo que sigue, quiero retomar la cuestión de cómo tratamos a los pacientes con trastornos de “pseudo-self” haciendo dos cosas:

reanimar el contexto en el que el análisis de defensas y las intervenciones no interpretativas se contraponen continuamente, y

volver sobre mi ejemplo clínico para especificar el abanico de trabajo que estas condiciones requieren.

Comenzaré con algo que ocurrió durante mi tratamiento con Casey. Ya he mencionado que era una talentosa profesora de música de unos 45 años, pero ahora quiero explorar su relación con la música con mayor profundidad porque creo que nos dice algo importante sobre la compleja interrelación entre la disociación como mecanismo autorregulador y las demás operaciones defensivas de la mente. Un dato significativo del entorno familiar de Casey es que su madre era una música consagrada y de cierto renombre, al igual que su abuela materna. Todas estas mujeres asistieron al mismo

CONTACT Gila Ashtor doctorashtor@gmail.com

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

El Pseudo-Self: Disociación y Trastornos del Sentir

Gila Ashtor PHD L.P.

Gila Ashtor

239 Central Park West, #1BE, Nueva York, NY 10024, USA
doctorashtor@gmail.com

Gila Ashtor   Profesora Adjunta de Psicoanálisis Clínico en la Universidad de Columbia. También forma parte de la facultad del Programa de Posdoctorado en Psicoanálisis y Psicoterapia de la Universidad de Nueva York y de IPTAR. Es autora de tres libros: Homo Psyche: On Queer Theory and Erotophobia (Fordham UP, 2021), que recibió el Premio Alan Bray de la MLA al mejor libro y fue finalista del Lambda Literary Award; Exigent Psychoanalysis: The Interventions of Jean Laplanche (Routledge, 2021), y Aural History (Punctum, 2020). Actualmente trabaja en un libro sobre los trastornos del sentir —fenómenos como el gaslighting, el consentimiento vacío y las relaciones tóxicas— que combina su experiencia interdisciplinaria. Sus principales áreas de especialización académica y clínica incluyen los trastornos disociativos, el trauma y la sexualidad. Ejerce en consulta privada en la ciudad de Nueva York.

Hay un tipo depaciente que acecha al psicoanálisis desde los márgenes, que nuncatermina de asentarse en las categorías diagnósticas existentes perotampoco desaparece del todo. Diversamente denominado “normótico”, “falso self” o personalidad “como si”, este pacientedesconcierta a los esquemas clasificatorios convencionales porque,aunque aparentemente es de alto funcionamiento, verbal, cooperativo,con el juicio de realidad intacto y sin necesariamente un historialsignificativo de trauma, acaba revelándose que algo fundamental —lacapacidad de sentir sus propios sentimientos— está ausente, esdisfuncional o inexistente. Detectar el problema puede ser casiimposible, no solo porque una “ausencia” es su rasgo definitorio,sino también porque el paciente rara vez se queja de ello (de nosentir sus sentimientos). Mientras que, en circunstancias ordinarias,las insatisfacciones emocionales del paciente son el motor queimpulsa el tratamiento —por más ambivalente e inconsistente quesea ese impulso—, en estos casos el paciente padeceprecisamente un problema mecánico de este tipo: una desconexión delos sentimientos auténticos que hace que la conciencia de lainsatisfacción sea esquiva e improbable.En lo que sigue, mecentro en el “pseudo-self”: una variación particular deorganización disociativa en la que la normalidad y la funcionalidadcamuflan la ausencia de autenticidad emocional. Exploro la conexiónentre esta organización de personalidad única y el actual régimenneoliberal, donde la desconexión, la velocidad y la eficiencia sonensalzadas como las características óptimas de la persona exitosa.La persistente incapacidad de nuestro campo para enfrentar losproblemas generalizados del “pseudo-self” nos hace cómplices deuna cultura dañada, deshumanizante y desconectada.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Disociación: Defensa y/o Déficit en la Formación de un Pseudo-Self

Anne Alvarez PHD

Anne Alvarez

Londres, UK
annealvarez380@gmail.com

Anne Alvarez   Psicoterapeuta infantil y de adolescentes consultora (Co-Coordinadora retirada del Servicio de Autismo) en la Tavistock Clinic, Londres. Es autora de Live Company y coeditora de Autism and Personality. Being Alive: Building on the Work of Anne Alvarez (ed. J. Edwards) fue publicado en 2002. También es autora de The Thinking Heart (2012).

Esta discusión ofrece un comentario al importante artículo de Gila Ashtor, que aborda la dificultad de tratar y comprender a pacientes con una personalidad normótica o“como si” que, en realidad, presentan un grado considerable de disociación. El paciente que describe Ashtor no parece estar gravemente disociado, pero parece incapaz—o no dispuesto— a seguir un pensamiento hasta llegar a otros pensamientos o sentimientos. Esta discusión cuestiona si esto es siempre tan defensivo como Ashtor piensa; es decir, si no podría tratarse más bien de una respuesta depresiva/deprivada frente a un objeto concebido no como malo o aterrador, sino como inadecuado y relativamente poco interesante. La consecuencia podría ser una respuesta apagada ante un objeto que suscita poco compromiso.

[[RunningHead]]

The Echo(Location) of Identity: Discussion of Tara Chivukula’s Trips of the Tongue

Velleda C. Ceccoli PHD

Velleda C. Ceccoli

New York, New York, USA
vceccoli@gmail.com

Velleda C. Ceccoli   Faculties of the NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, The Stephen Mitchell Center, and The Institute for Relational and Self Psychologies in Milan, Italy. She is also on the editorial boards of Psychoanalytic Dialogues and Studies in Gender and Sexuality. Velleda writes the ongoing psychoanalytic blog Out of My Mind and has published numerous journal articles on language, trauma, dissociation, sexuality, gender, and erotic experience. She maintains a private practice in New York.

In this essay, I examine how Tara Chivukula employs accents to investigate how culture and the languages we speak influence the formation of the self and our ongoing efforts to weave these influences into a coherent identity. My analysis traces a path from psychoanalytic theories developed by Kristeva, Loewald, and Bromberg, ultimately connecting these ideas to the poetic works of Glissant.

responseSet.title: The Pseudo-Self: Dissociation and Feeling Disorders
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Pseudo-Self: Dissociation and Feeling Disorders

Gila Ashtor PHD L.P.

Gila Ashtor

239 Central Park West, #1BE, 1234, New York, NY 10024, USA
doctorashtor@gmail.com

Gila Ashtor   Assistant Professor of Clinical Psychoanalysis at Columbia University. She is also on the Faculty at New York University’s Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy, and at IPTAR. She is the author of three books, Homo Psyche: On Queer Theory and Erotophobia (Fordham UP, 2021), which won the MLA’s Alan Bray Award for Best Book and was a finalist for the Lambda Literary Award, Exigent Psychoanalysis: The Interventions of Jean Laplanche (Routledge, 2021), and Aural History (Punctum, 2020). She is currently at work on a book about feeling disorders – phenomena such as gaslighting, empty consent, and toxic relationships – that combines her interdisciplinary expertise. Her primary areas of academic and clinical expertise include dissociative disorders, trauma and sexuality. She is in private practice in New York City.

There is a type of patient who haunts psychoanalysis from the margins, never settling into existing diagnostic categories but never completely disappearing either. Variously referred to as “normotic”, “false self”, and the “as if” personality, this patient confounds conventional classificatory schemas because while ostensibly high-functioning, verbal, cooperative, with reality-testing intact and not necessarily any major trauma history, it eventually emerges that something fundamental—the capacity to feel their feelings—is missing, dysfunctional, or non-existent. It can be nearly impossible to detect the problem, not least because an “absence” is its defining feature, but also because the patient rarely complains about the problem (of not feeling their feelings) either. Whereas under ordinary circumstances, the patient’s emotional dissatisfactions are the engine propelling treatment forward, however ambivalently and inconsistently, in these cases, the patient suffers from a mechanical problem of precisely this kind—a disconnection from authentic feelings—that makes the awareness of dissatisfaction elusive and unlikely. In what follows, I focus on the “pseudo-self”—a particular variation of dissociative organization in which normality and functionality camouflage the absence of emotional authenticity. I explore the connection between this unique personality organization and today’s neoliberal regime, where disconnection, speed, and efficiency are hailed as the optimal features of successful personhood. Our field’s continued inability to reckon with the pervasive problems of the “pseudo-self” makes us complicit with a damaged culture that is dehumanizing and disconnected.

Discussion of “Expansion and Regression in Oceanic Experience”

Lawrence Fischman MD

Lawrence Fischman

Boston, Massachusetts, USA
lfisch55@gmail.com

Lawrence Fischman   Psychiatrist and psychoanalyst who practiced in New York and Maine for many years. In addition to a full-time private practice, he taught as a Clinical Instructor in Psychiatry at New York-Presbyterian/Weill Cornell Medical Center and later at Tufts University School of Medicine’s Maine Track Program, where he still serves on the Admissions subcommittee. In 1983, he published a paper comparing psychedelic drug states with dreams and psychosis in Schizophrenia Bulletin. Since 2019, he has published several papers dealing with psychedelics and psychoanalysis beginning with Seeing without self: Discovering new meaning with psychedelic-assisted psychotherapy. He is currently writing a paper on psychedelics and music called Becoming the Music.

In Expansion and Regression in Oceanic Experience, Peoples and Rundel examine “what happens from a psychoanalytic perspective when a patient engages in psychedelic-assisted therapy.” In an elegant paper that has both a clinical and theoretical focus, they discuss what Romain Rolland described metaphorically as the “oceanic feeling.” They believe such feelings can have transformative value, in contrast with what they see as a prevailing tendency among psychoanalysts, including me, to view such experiences as indications of pathological fusion and unhealthy regression. Here, I clarify my own relational perspective on unitive feelings and apply this to the case vignette offered in Peoples and Rundel’s paper. I highlight convergences and contrasts between this relational perspective and the far-reaching model of levels of consciousness proposed by Peoples and Rundel.

responseSet.title: Between Restraint and Vertigo: Notes from the Fire Escape
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Between Restraint and Vertigo: Notes from the Fire Escape

Jade McGleughlin L.I.C.S.W.

Jade McGleughlin

180 Peal St., Cambridge, MA 02139, USA
Jademcg2@gmail.com

Jade McGleughlin   Past President, Supervisor and a Supervising Analyst and Faculty member at The Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis. She is on the editorial board of Psychoanalytic Dialogues and Studies in Gender in Sexuality, and is in private Practice in Cambridge, MA providing consultation, supervision, psychotherapy and psychoanalysis to children and adults. Her book Clinical Storytelling, Art and the Problems of Being; The Analyst’s Necessary Vertigo was published by Routledge in 2024. She is a portrait painter.

This discussion engages Sam Guzzardi’s Laplanche in the Consulting Room: An Emerging Paradigm for Psychotherapeutic Action to consider how analytic transformation unfolds between restraint and vertigo, containment and dissolution. I take up Laplanche’s language of metabolism, transference, and the sole hermeneut while pressing on the edges where the analyst’s implication unsettles neat distinctions between frame and field, center and periphery. What emerges is an argument for nonsovereignty as an ethic of staying with simultaneity – where binding and unbinding, danger and transformation, intimacy and dislocation occur at once, leaving the analyst and patient altered in ways that may never resolve into representation.

Ecstasy and Agony: When Too Much is Not Enough

Janine de Peyer L.C.S.W.-R.

Janine de Peyer

425 East 86th Street, Suite 1D, New York, NY 10028, USA
jdepeyer@gmail.com

Janine de Peyer   Supervising, training analyst and faculty at the National Institute for the Psychotherapies, New York. She is also faculty at the Florida Psychoanalytic Center, and the Stephen Mitchell Relational Study Center. De Peyer is Associate Editor with Psychoanalytic Dialogues: The International Journal of Relational Perspectives, and has presented internationally on transference-countertransference, eroticism, dissociation, and uncanny communication in the therapeutic dyad. Publications include: “Piercing the Veil: From Intuition to Telepathic Receptivity” (2025), “Unspoken Rhapsody: Female Erotic Countertransference and the Dissociation of Desire” (2022), and “Uncanny Communication and the Porous Mind” (2016). She is in private practice in New York City where she integrates EMDR within a relational psychoanalytic framework.

This paper, cast in the form of reverie, delves into the emotional currents, bodily sensations, and inner dialogues that may surface for an analyst drawn into the powerful transference – countertransference dynamics stirred by a patient’s compulsive eroticism. When early maternal deprivation and abandonment give rise to adult sexualization as a defense against more primal anguish—grief, longing, and existential solitude—the analyst’s own sexuality may be unconsciously summoned as a potential vehicle for healing. In following the difficult trajectory of Schoen’s treatment with Luke, I imagine the inner turbulence such a process might awaken in any analyst—a fraught interplay of frustration, unexpected surges of sexual arousal, and a deep undertow of self doubt—hallmarks of the complex, erotically charged analytic encounter.

Analytic Eroticism: Commentary on Schoen’s “Erotic Entanglements”

Dianne Elise PHD

Dianne Elise

5349 College Avenue, Oakland, California 94618, USA
elised@earthlink.net

Dianne Elise   Personal and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; a Training Analyst, International Psychoanalytic Association, and has served on the Editorial Boards of the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association and Studies in Gender and Sexuality. Her over 40 psychoanalytic publications include journal articles and book chapters on a wide range of subjects. Elise’s 2019 book with Routledge, Creativity and the Erotic Dimensions of the Analytic Field – featured in a New Books in Psychoanalysis podcast, August, 2024– expands her work in innovative ways and presents her contemporary thinking on erotic life in psychoanalysis. Her two most recent publications, Creative becoming: Response to Palmer’s “Aesthetic position” and The sounds of silence: Embodied registers, erotic reverie, emphasize the role of libidinal vitality in clinical treatment. She is in private practice in Oakland, California.

I highlight the ways in which a patient’s infant sexual history can manifest with palpable impact within the co-created analytic field. When maternal eroticism has not been plentiful and well attuned, significant clinical challenges ensue. A clinician’s embodied, libidinal capacity for analytic eroticism is essential in engaging a patient at the level of the maternal erotic matrix. A mutual encounter in facing loss is imperative in stimulating psychic growth.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Who Gets to Be a Child? Clinical and Cultural Reflections on Racism, White Infantilization, and Kyle Rittenhouse

David G. Cushman Psy.D.

David G. Cushman

1453 Mission St, San Francisco, CA 94103, USA
davidcushmanpsyd@gmail.com

David G. Cushman   Licensed clinical psychologist. He is a core faculty member of the PsyD program at CIIS and has a private practice in Oakland, CA, where he sees children, teens, adults and couples. He worked in community mental health for several years as a clinician, supervisor, and program manager. He has written, presented, and published on community mental health, Whiteness and racism, and professional basketball, all from a psychoanalytic perspective.

This paper began from the trial of Kyle Rittenhouse and my disturbing reaction to his testimony. In an attempt to understand my response, I examine both adultification bias, where black youth are seen as older than their age and more threatening than their white peers, and Whiteness from a psychoanalytic lens. Using psychoanalytic theory, social psychology, ethnography research, cultural examples, and my own self-reflections, I suggest a new term, White Infantilization, that describes how white youth are viewed as innocent and young far beyond when they should be and the costs associated with this. I use two case examples, featuring a black and white teen, where themes of adultification bias and White Infantilization are prominent, to explore the relevance of these ideas for current clinical practice.

Amplifying Cushman’s Adultification and Infantilization

Marsha H. Levy-Warren PHD

Marsha H. Levy-Warren

415 Central Park West, Suite 1EL, New York, NY 10025, USA
mlevywarren@aol.com

Marsha H. Levy-Warren   Clinical psychologist and psychoanalyst who writes, teaches, lectures, and consults both nationally and internationally. She is the author of The Adolescent Journey (Jason Aronson, 1996; reissued by Rowman and Littlefield, 2004), and numerous articles on clinical and developmental theory, adolescence, and various aspects of culture.

This discussion uses Dr Cushman’s ideas about adultification and infantilization as a jumping off point for thinking about the nuanced ways in which these concepts may influence development. It offers a number of clinical anecdotes to describe both their use as Dr. Cushman proposes and the expansion of their use into other aspects of maturation and development. It suggests thinking of them as independent concepts, each applicable on a continuum of influence, rather than the inverse of one another. It emphasizes the importance of taking into account class, race, gender, sexuality, and geography in employing ideas about adultification and infantilization.

Erotic Entanglements in the Search to Be and Belong: Traversing the Forcefield of Dependency and Desire

Sarah Schoen PHD

Sarah Schoen

1133 Broadway, Suite 1600, New York, NY 10010, USA
seschoen@me.com

Sarah Schoen   Faculty, Supervising, and Training Analyst at the William Alanson White Institute, Adjunct Clinical Professor of Psychology at the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, and Invited Faculty at the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research. She is Executive Editor at Psychoanalytic Dialogues, on the editorial board of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, and co-editor of Patriarchy and its Discontents: Psychoanalytic Perspectives (2023). She is in private practice in Manhattan’s Flatiron District.

This paper takes up the clinical challenges of working with patients for whom the repetitive pursuit of intense, erotic relationships both express and conceal yearnings to be and belong. While sexual experiences can elicit core fantasies of transformation, awareness of the very otherness that offers such promise also threatens a fragile self and is thus negated. A detailed clinical account illuminates sexuality’s dense entanglement with experiences of coming alive and the knotted strands of character armor, sexual charge, developmental trauma, disintegrative anxieties, and concretized longings for physical touch that permeate eroticism in the space of breakdown. The author takes up how these unstable intersections contribute to patients’ and analysts’ fears and avoidance of engaging these entanglements in the analytic relationship with its inherent seductions and frustrations; at the same time, its through delving into and living through these overlaps that the basic issues of being and becoming can emerge.

This reply to the commentaries elaborates analysts’ anxieties about their own erotic embodiment as well as their inhibitions in integrating the maternal and the erotic. These issues are explored as they relate to the intricate connections between dependency and sexuality. The author emphasizes that the inter-implication between these domains is at its most clinically challenging with patients who most need a psychoanalysis to address these very links.

Possibilities and Expansion for Adultification Bias and White Infantilization

David G. Cushman Psy.D.

David G. Cushman

1453 Mission St, San Francisco, CA 94103, USA
davidcushmanpsyd@gmail.com

David G. Cushman   Licensed clinical psychologist. He is a core faculty member of the PsyD program at CIIS and has a private practice in Oakland, CA, where he sees children, teens, adults, and couples. He worked in community mental health for several years as a clinician, supervisor, and program manager. He has written, presented, and published on community mental health, Whiteness and racism, and professional basketball, all from a psychoanalytic perspective.

In examining both Levy-Warren and Suslovic’s commentaries, I explore the different ways they engage in adultification bias and White infantilization, and how each brought out important considerations about these ideas. In particular, I examine the potential meaning of the common belief that centering race and Whiteness can be unnuanced, how the legacy of racism and colonialism has been present in psychoanalysis since its founding, and how state violence and genocide limit any possibility of childhood. Finally, I take up Levy-Warren and Suslovic’s wonderful clinical vignette and explore their richness and complexities.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obscura/Lucida: The Punctum of the Queer Image

Charlotte Curtis L.C.S.W.-R.

Charlotte Curtis

139 Fulton St, RM 1004, New York, NY 10038, USA
charlottecurtis.lcsw@gmail.com

Charlotte Curtis   Psychoanalyst in private practice in New York City. She is a graduate of the Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity, the Stephen Mitchell Relational Study Center, and the Smith College School for Social Work. She is currently a teaching faculty member at IPSS and ICP, both in New York City.

Inspired by patients who present childhood photographs as part of adult treatment, this essay considers how unformulated elements of the queer and trans childhood enter (or, resist entering) the psychoanalytic field. While some childhoods are readily recallable and memories abound, often there is a present absence – a no “there-there” – and the unknowable past seeks expression in creative ways. Informed by actual photographs and scenes that come alive in treatment, the analyst’s imagistic reverie may serve as a portal to the Otherness of early queer and trans self-experience, which had to be dissociated in the face of traumatic negation and nonrecognition by the cis/heteronormative social order. Centering on the photographic image and Barthes’s concept of punctum, this essay will consider the generative power of the negative, the analyst’s private space, and ideas on reverie. If we allow ourselves to see the queer child, to let them hover in imagistic reverie, there is an invitation for recognition, in afterwardsness. This essay wonders: Can the eye listen in ways that the ear cannot?

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Head Cinema, Transitional Space, and the Archive of the Analyst

Ken Corbett PHD

Ken Corbett

30 E. 21st Street, New York, NY, USA
kencorbett@kencorbett.com

Ken Corbett   Professor, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. He is the author of “Boyhoods: Rethinking Masculinities,” and “A Murder Over a Girl: Justice, Gender, Junior High.”

Curtis’s ideas about head cinema are discussed in relation to the temporal, contingent, and transitory nature of photographing distinct from the photograph. The act of photographing is considered in relation to transference – a phenomenon that relies on the creation of transitional space. The ways in which the analyst – as archivist – holds aesthetic experiences like head cinemas are also explored.

[[RunningHead]]

The Moonkeepers

June Lee Kwon PHD

June Lee Kwon

30 E 21st Street, Suite 2A1, New York, NY 10010
juneleekwon@gmail.com

June Lee Kwon   Clinical psychologist in private practice in New York City. She received her doctoral training in Adelphi University, and she is currently in training to become a psychoanalyst in New York University’s Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. She serves as a board director at the Foundation for Community Psychoanalysis in Brooklyn, NY.

The present paper is a response to Curtis’s essay reflecting on the phenomenon of patients presenting childhood photographs and other images. In response to Curtis’s moving accounts of the images her and her patients bring up, I offer four vignettes written with poetic prose to facilitate deeper reflection on “unformulated” (“unframed”) experiences related to the intersections of gender, sexuality, and race. I also offer highlights on dyadic relationships portrayed on photographs between the photographer and the subject (object–subject, and vice versa) that comes alive in the consulting room, and in Curtis’s and, potentially, readers’ “head cinema.’’

In this brief essay, I respond to the illuminations and reveries of my discussants, Corbett and Kwon. Using metaphors found in the 1956 epic science-fiction poem, Aniara, I offer further thoughts (and pose further questions) on the interplay between head cinema, psychoanalytic sensing, and the punctum of the current sociopolitical moment.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Expansion and Regression: Reply to Discussions

Karen Peoples PHD and Megan Rundel PHD

Megan Rundel

San Francisco, California, USA

3824 Grand Avenue Oakland, CA 94610, USA
meganrundel@gmail.com

Megan Rundel   Clinical Psychologist and a Personal and Supervising Analyst and Faculty at the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California. She is also a graduate of the Psychedelic Assisted Therapies and Research program at the California Institute of Integral Studies, as well as a number of ketamine training programs. She has taught and written widely on the use of psychedelics in enhancing psychoanalysis; her most recent paper is “Psychedelic Psychoanalysis: Transformations of the Self,” published in Psychoanalytic Dialogues in 2022. She maintains a private practice of psychoanalysis and consultation in Oakland, California.

We are pleased and grateful to have such a rich set of responses to our paper, which challenge our premises and creatively elaborate on them. We stand by our contention that recent writings in the nascent field of psychedelic-assisted psychoanalysis take too far analogies between oceanic states of consciousness and early childhood mentation, and thus prejudice how oceanic states are seen. We attempt to clarify confusion about the different meanings of regression and further distinguish regression from expansion. We support the practical clinical advice offered when working therapeutically with expanded states of consciousness and agree with the importance of stabilizing and integrating witnessing and perspective-taking capacities. Finally, we appreciate the dilemma of language in discussing experiences like oneness states that generate a “feeling of the sacred,” and clarify our reason for avoiding saturated terms that suggest religious or spiritual meanings.

responseSet.title: Attacks on Linking: Musings on Psychic Survival
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Attacks on Linking: Musings on Psychic Survival

Holly Levenkron LCSW L.I.C.S.W.

Holly Levenkron

Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA
hollylevenkron@gmail.com

Holly Levenkron  

Ring the bells that still can ring

Forget your perfect offering

There is a crack, a crack in everything

That’s how the light gets in.

– Leonard Cohen (Anthem)

Attacks on Linking: Musings on Psychic Survival

Current US leadership lands us in a living, breathing “Attack on Linking;” a dark turning point in our history with catastrophic processes creating challenges to democracy. Bion understood catastrophic attacks on the psychic apparatus occurring when the self is linked with “unknowable and unthinkable” realities; attacks on linking impede the psyche’s ability to symbolize and metabolize these realities (Bion, 1959). With America’s authoritarianism, what seems to be a rising fascism, we see freedoms coopted into dark, threatening aesthetics. This reflects Walter Benjamin’s condemnation of fascism as “… the triumph of a false aesthetics over ethical and human values” (Stewart, 2010). As the current administration severs links to whatever threatens it; this is clearly an attack on linking on a societal level, to the threat Democracy poses.

Self-Silencing

Analysts have seen trauma states threaten cohesion, even survival of the self, when paranoid projections, too difficult to bear, are expelled. Attacks, on links to present reality, render the self incapable of integrating thoughts, feelings and ideals. When trauma interrupts pathways to ideals and vitality, shame states and self-silencing intrude (Morrison, 2009); psychic organization is halted, ultimately impeding psychic survival. Within linear registers of self-annihilation the living breathing container that once held us becomes brittle.

My patient John’s family had endured an oppressive authoritarian, political regime. Living in the US, now observing mandates of “un-freedoms” from the new administration, he’s become progressively depressed, vulnerable and terrified. I had not picked up the extremes in his narrative until recently when in a trancelike way he shared: “The sun was going down, darkness moved in. I knew where I was, yet unable to make out forms, shapes and objects – where minutes before I knew they were there.” Then John yelled, “This sounds psychotic”! I said, “It doesn’t, I get it. It sounds like you’re unable to believe what you’re experiencing – like losing a life support … air that we breathe!” I thought of disclosing my reactions to US events but worried my realness could disturb him. How vulnerable was he – how close up could I get? I was hoping to offer not quite support but more, emotional participation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

My Mother’s Haiti, Redux

Shari Appollon L.C.S.W.-R.

Shari Appollon

USA
147 Prince Street #201, Brooklyn, NY 11201
shari@talktoshari.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3839-532X

Shari Appollon   Haitian-American psychoanalyst in private practice in Brooklyn New York. She is a 2024 graduate of the National Institute for the Psychotherapies and a 2019 graduate of the OYP at The Stephen Mitchell Relational Study Center. She currently serves as the Associate Director of Clinical Services at NYC Affirmative Psychotherapy group practice. Shari is the recipient of NIP’s 2020 Educator’s Award for her paper “The Triple Entendre” and runner-up for Division 39’s 2020 Candidate Essay Contest for her paper “The Parable of the Sower.” She writes primarily about culture, psychoanalytic theory and enjoys engaging in herbalism in her free time.

Throughout the paper I acknowledge and contend with the impact of transgenerational trauma and love as it pertains to the manifest reality of one’s country of origin, while simultaneously questioning the unconscious elements of melancholia. I build on concepts from David Eng & Shinhee Han’s seminal paper “A Dialogue on Racial Melancholia” through the lens of a first-generation Haitian-American. Twenty-five years post their publication in Psychoanalytic Dialogues how far has psychoanalytic theory expanded in addressing the concept of melancholia as a depathologized structure of everyday group experience for immigrants? How have the voices of prominent psychoanalysts added to the conversation of transgenerational trauma and love, the immigrant experience and ethnocultural impact on identity formation as it pertains to what one deems as “home”? I will teeter between experience-near dynamics with my mother and maternal grandmother, along with the treatment of a severely depressed Haitian-American woman, highlighting the flow of melancholia throughout the paper.

[[RunningHead]]

Reflections on Boyhood as a Non/Human State

Brianna Suslovic MSW

Brianna Suslovic

969 E. 60th Street, Chicago, IL 60637
bsuslovic@uchicago.edu
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-1723-4586

Brianna Suslovic   Psychoanalytic social worker and Ph.D. candidate based in Chicago, Illinois. She holds an MSW from Smith College and a Bachelor’s degree from Harvard College. The majority of her research and practice experience has been in and around the criminal-legal system.

Through a cultural-historical contextualization of boyhood and the figure of the Child, this reply offers a set of theoretical queries in response to Cushman’s theoretical intervention, white infantilization. This reply begins with a discussion of the racist and colonial legacies of early psychoanalytic theory, developing this narrative into a broader discussion of how infantilization is simultaneously used as a tool to protect stereotypical white innocence while promoting stereotypical Black ignorance. Through a discussion of protectionist impulses that are associated with the figure of the Child as white and innocent, this reply advances a question about unchilding in the global political context, asking: what happens when the state is the perpetrator of white infantilization and nonwhite dehumanization? This response concludes with a discussion of clinical material that captures racial and gendered dynamics of infantilization, meditating on possibilities contained within Cushman’s theoretical interventions.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Courageous Transformative Moments in Treatment: A Response to Rina Lazar

Sandra Buechler PHD

Sandra Buechler

20 West 74th Street, New York, NY 10023, USA
sbuechler2@msn.com

Sandra Buechler   Training and Supervising Analyst at the William Alanson White Institute. She is the author of Clinical Values: Emotions that Guide Psychoanalytic Treatment (Analytic Press, 2004), Making a Difference in Patients’ Lives, (Routledge, 2008), which won the Gradiva award, Still Practicing: The Heartaches and Joys of a Clinical Career, (Routledge, 2012), Understanding and Treating Patients in Clinical Psychoanalysis: Lessons from Literature (Routledge, 2015) Psychoanalytic Reflections: Training and Practice, (IPBooks, 2017) Psychoanalytic Approaches to Problems in Living, (Routledge, 2019), Poetic Dialogues (IPBooks, 2021) and Erich Fromm: A Contemporary Introduction (Routledge, 2024).

In moving, poetic prose, Rina Lazar explores her concept of “telescopic memories,” which she describes as “… vivid memories from different times that flood our psyche abruptly, bubbling up in our mind …” Lazar makes inspiring use of her own memories in her treatment of a man at a significant crossroad in his life. My discussion compares Lazar’s concept with ideas from the study of poetry. I also describe my clinical experiences of patients who carry a vision of the life their “unharmed self” would have lived, had they not suffered traumatic abuse.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Trips of the Tongue: Accents in the Consulting Room

Tara Chivukula LCSW

Tara Chivukula

USA
1500 West 38th St. Suite 31, Austin, TX 78731
gayatri104@yahoo.com

Tara Chivukula   Graduate of the Manhattan Institute for Psychoanalysis, Inc. (MIP). She teaches and supervises in the One-Year Certificate Program in Psychoanalysis & the Sociopolitical World at MIP and at the Institute of Contemporary Psychotherapy & Psychoanalysis Fellowship Program (D.C.). She previously served on the board of Austin Psychoanalytic (AP) and is a current mentor to early-career clinicians through the AP mentorship program. Her clinical specialization focus on bicultural individuals and their experience of identity, transition, belonging, and cultural dislocation. She is in private practice in Austin, TX, and New York City.

While the psychoanalytic literature is plentiful on the topic of language, there is relatively little focus on accents. Accents reveal (and conceal) our class, caste, ethnicity, and place of origin. I argue that an analyst’s and patient’s accents play a vital role in the transference matrix. This is illustrated through the case study of a British patient and various South Asian patients. Two films are analyzed through the lens of colonialism, race, and class. Accents as markers of status, reinvention, and third individuation are studied in light of my family’s immigration history and personal identity.

[[RunningHead]]

Reply: Speaking in Tongues: Notes on a Linguistic Trip

Tara Chivukula LCSW

Tara Chivukula

USA
1500 West 38th St. Suite 31, Austin, TX 78731
gayatri104@yahoo.com

Tara Chivukula   Graduate of the Manhattan Institute for Psychoanalysis, Inc. (MIP). She teaches and supervises in the One-Year Certificate Program in Psychoanalysis & the Sociopolitical World at MIP and at the Institute of Contemporary Psychotherapy & Psychoanalysis Fellowship Program (D.C.). She previously served on the board of Austin Psychoanalytic (AP) and is a current mentor to early-career clinicians through the AP mentorship program. Her clinical specialization focus is on bicultural individuals and their experience of identity, transition, belonging, and cultural dislocation. She is in private practice in Austin, TX, and New York City.

This article responds to the commentaries of Ceccoli, Csillag, and Saba. Across the articles, language emerges as a sonic bridge between the body and the symbolic. Experiences of separation, loss, and the desire for belonging and recognition are explored through the realm of sound and its connection to class and cultural identity. I argue that accents appear as expressions of lalangue, the jouissance of early care, emerging from the habitus of the body ego and shaped by normative unconscious processes. Finally, I reflect on the ambivalence between hybridity and loss, suggesting that not all divisions can be reconciled.

Tuning in to the Sounds of Aliveness

Sharone Bergner PHD FIPA

Sharone Bergner

139 West 82nd Street, Suite 1CD, New York, NY 10024, USA
sbergnerphd@gmail.com

Sharone Bergner   Teaching faculty and is a clinical consultant in the Contemporary Freudian track at the NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy, a member of and former faculty at the Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research, and a member of the International Psychoanalytic Association. She serves on the editorial board of The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, and is in full-time private practice of psychoanalysis, psychotherapy, and clinical supervision in New York City.

This paper takes up Lombardi’s writing about facilitating the relationship with the concrete body and an over-privileging of working in the transference in relation to both the clinical domain and the domain of psychoanalytic thinking, in which the body has lacked sufficient elaboration. I explore a parallel between patients’ mind-body dissociations and the absence of the body as body in psychoanalytic thinking, suggesting the latter is rooted in a disavowal of uncontrollable physicality, which undergirds a tilt toward the symbolic. Using Winnicott’s depiction of processes of psyche-soma integration toward indwelling, I situate Lombardi’s clinical position relative to an exploration of a noisy versus quiet engagement of parental reverie with the child’s body and its physical handling, and discuss Lombardi’s work addressing the meeting of the representational realm and the concrete body as catalyzing a developmental thrust toward integration and, as such, as a particular kind of self-other relation.

The discussant introduces four models of mind to shape theory and practice around embodied experience. The discussant locates several ways in which Lombardi’s offering fits into one or more of the four models described. The author compares and contrasts the differences between the approach to embodied experience offered by Lombardi and that of the author with a brief clinical illustration.

Katie Gentile   Professor of Interdisciplinary Studies at John Jay College of Criminal Justice (City University of New York). She is the author of Creating bodies: Eating disorders as self-destructive survival and the 2017 Gradiva Award winning The Business of being made: The temporalities of reproductive technologies, in psychoanalysis and cultures, both from Routledge. She is editor of the journal Studies in Gender and Sexuality, on the faculty of New York University’s Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and the Psychoanalysis and the Critical Social Psychology program at the CUNY Graduate Center.

This paper is a discussion of Riccardo Lombardi’s paper, Transference onto the body: Beyond the relational, the symbolic and hystorical. Leaning on previous psychoanalytic writers who focused on the body as a cultural reproduction, as well as critical theory expanding the reaches of race, class, gender, and sexuality, this discussion aims to problematize reductive ideas of temporality that linearize time and space, reproducing the violent Enlightenment binaries of body/mind undergirding colonial violence. Focusing on the clinical and theoretical ideas presented by Lombardi, this discussion applies a theory of dense temporality emergent through tensegrity, within which sensation and the body are not opportunities for a minded containment but the expanse of an environmentally co-emergent being.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In This Issue, Psychoanalytic Dialogues 35–2

Stephen Hartman PHD , Lauren Levine PHD and Amy Cooney PHD

Amy Cooney

Amy Cooney  

As the result of many hours of conversation about ethics among publishers, editors, authors, and representatives from search engines whose aggregated links promote the growth of our field, an important policy shift has taken place that concerns clinical writing. For many years, patient confidentiality was safeguarded by a standard of consent. Currently, the gold standard is a set of very specific guidelines for anonymization that each journal adapts to best fit its mission. Patient consent is still permitted and even encouraged, but now not required. In other words, it’s up to the author as to how to approach the task of writing about patients, as long the material is heavily anonymized, including the option of sharing the paper with the patient.

This shift has raised several important and generative questions about what makes psychoanalytic writing clinical? In years past, it was common to hear certain narratives referred to as “clinical” papers, others as “theory papers,” and still others concerning “the social.” We’ve worked to complicate these distinctions in Dialogues because we see no merit in pigeonholing authors and their papers along these lines. Plus, what makes a narrative clinical and what makes it something else is itself a theoretical question that is best answered in the ineffable and emergent space where clinical moments, rendered in the analyst-author’s voice, meet the reader’s read.

As Thomas Ogden (2005) explained: “Analytic writing constitutes a literary genre of its own. It involves the linking of an analytic idea with an analytic experience created in the medium of language. What makes this literary genre so demanding is that experience-‐including analytic experience‐-does not come to us in words. This fact generates a paradox that lies at the core of analytic writing: analytic experience (which cannot be said or written) must be transformed into ‘fiction’ (an imaginative rendering of experience in words) in order to convey to the reader something of what is true to the emotional experience that the analyst had with the patient.

responseSet.title: Calamities and Secrets, the Power of the Fetish
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Calamities and Secrets, the Power of the Fetish

Margaret Rubin PHD

Margaret Rubin

Los Angeles, California, USA
1006 Pine St, Santa Monica, CA 90405-3924, USA
margrubin@gmail.com

Margaret Rubin   Member and senior faculty at New Center of Psychoanalysis (NCP) in Los Angeles, California. She graduated from Southern California Psychoanalytic Institute and Society in 2000 and was awarded the Thesis of the Year. She has been awarded faculty of the year at NCP on two occasions for teaching Relational Theory to the psychoanalytic candidates. Dr. Rubin has been published in Contemporary Psychoanalysis and Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

This paper examines the developmental consequences of a fractured, self-maintained and held together by physical restraints, which over time morphed into an unusual fetish that dominated S’s life. Although initially lifesaving by giving him the illusion of power over his tormentors—primarily his psychotic mother whose unleashed, manic sexual behaviors imprinted his mind and body—his fetish kept him embedded in a fixed and repetitive pattern of living in a frozen past that obfuscated a future of new possibilities. Over fits and starts to his seven-year analysis, S was able to leave the safety of his universe of one for the unpredictability of the relational world—no small feat given the enactment that almost ended his treatment. S’s split off affective experiences were accessed through the medium of his remarkable dreams. As our relationship deepened, he was able to open his arms to the present and changing future.

responseSet.title: Reflections on Margaret Rubin’s Analysis of Saturated, Sexualized Horror
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reflections on Margaret Rubin’s Analysis of Saturated, Sexualized Horror

Paul Williams PHD

Paul Williams

San Francisco, California, USA
36 Via La Cumbre, Greenbrae, San Francisco, CA 94904, USA
pwilliams.eu@outlook.com

Paul Williams   Psychoanalyst with The British Psychoanalytical Society before moving to the US. He was Joint Editor-in-Chief of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis with Glen Gabbard between 2001 and 2007. He has published widely on the subject of severe disturbance and psychosis, including ‘Invasive Objects: minds under siege’ (Routledge) and with Murray Jackson, ‘Unimaginable Storms: a search for meaning in psychosis’ (Karnac), and ‘The Fifth Principle’ Trilogy (Routledge). A novel, ‘Nothing Happened’ (Sul Books) will appear in 2026.

This discussion reflects on the internal object world of S, ravaged by erotized narcissism, emotional violence and neglect, and his desperate attempts to stay psychically alive in isolation from a very early age. He had too much and too little to bring to his analysis, having experienced brutalization in a part-object world of non-selves or non-persons, but his dreams seem to have acted as a lifeline, a halfway house between his inner experience of alienation and torture and the profound need to be seen if personhood were to ever develop. He could not think, but he could feel, albeit mostly in agony, and his dreams communicated this. Through careful attention to the countertransference, his analyst helps him to paint his own picture of the horror he endured. and, without intrusion or premature reaching for “improvement,” his analyst’s acceptance and growing understanding of who the soul-murdered child S might have been, allows him to glimpse and begin entry into a world of tender reciprocity around his existential nightmare. The analyst’s technique is a model of how to combine patience, tolerance of uncertainty and focus in the treatment of severe disturbance.

She is in private practice in Tel Aviv, Israel.

The discussion of Rubin’s clinical case of a severely fetishistic patient is an invitation to delve into the years-long challenge of reinventing psychoanalysis for severe perversion, and more generally, into the challenge of extending the reach of psychoanalytic treatment to more disturbed patients and traumatic states of mind. Based on her clinical psychoanalytic experience and inspired mainly by the radical late writings of Winnicott and Bion on early breakdown and mental catastrophe, Eshel offers theoretical-clinical new possibilities of analytic presence and the ensuing patient-analyst deep interconnectedness, which forges a fundamental ontological-experiential dimension in psychoanalytic work. She focuses on the unique challenges encountered by analysts/therapists in going beyond epistemological (knowing) exploration and interpretation to being and becoming in the experience, interconnected with the patient’s psychic reality; a two-in-oneness that may deepen into being at-one-with the patient’s innermost traumatic, unrepressed and unrepresented, non-communicating emotional reality. The critical idea is that patient-with-analyst or patient-at-one-with-analyst, psyche-with-psyche, may undergo the devastating experience that made it impossible for the patient to be there and experience it alone. In being within the patient’s deeply unbearable states, patient-with-analyst are given a new chance to relive and break through massive traumatic impacts and catastrophic loneliness. This way of working also resonates with the ontological shift currently taking place in psychoanalysis. Finally, regarding the patient’s death in this analysis, Eshel reflects on the dangers inherent in this psychoanalytic work of reaching into and activating catastrophic depths of psychic traumatization, so that the change of deadness-aliveness in treatment is radical.

I am presenting my responses to the discussants individually as each discussion is written from a different perspective. In each one, I address the author’s associations and queries that prodded me to reconsider certain questions I had asked myself, or, neglected to ask, thus deepening my understanding of S and the immense suffering he endured. Taking these three commentaries individually and collectively creates a rich tapestry of S’s kaleidoscopic selves, evolving from his pseudo self to his developing real self.

Après Coup of “Romantic Bonds, Binds, and Ruptures: Couples on the Brink”

Virginia Goldner PHD , Lauren Levine PHD and Stephen Hartman PHD

Stephen Hartman

Stephen Hartman  

For this third Après Coup project, we revisit Virginia Goldner’s classic essay, Romantic Bonds, Binds and Ruptures: Couples on the Brink with discussions by Goldner and Mary Kim Brewster, Mary Joan Gerson, Marcela López González (in English and Spanish), Leigh Lyndon, Stacy Malin, Jean Malpas, Shelley Nathans, Phil Ringstrom, Robert Bartlett, and Paul and Ellen Wachtel.

Bob Bartlett

New York, NY 10027, USA
robertb4@optonline.net

Bob Bartlett   Private practice in New York City and White Plains, New York. He supervises in the Division of Psychology of Mount Sinai Health System. Dr. Bartlett, a psychologist and psychoanalyst, has worked with adults and couples for the last two decades.

In this response to Virginia Goldner’s 2016 article, Dr. Bartlett points to the still deeply relevant contributions offered by Goldner in considering the clinical and theoretical challenges of working with high conflict couples. Goldner’s emphasis on the therapist’s countertransference, her focus on the importance of attachment considerations, and her nuanced treatment of the effects of trauma on enactments in couples therapy all remain as meaningful now as they were in 2016.

In reflecting on Virginia Goldner’s, “Romantic bonds, binds, and ruptures: Couples on the brink,” the author acknowledges Goldner’s exceptionally astute observations of the intersubjective states of the therapist and couple, her rigorous and capacious synthesis of psychoanalysis, attachment, feminist, systems, and mentalization theories, and how the essay, claimed by psychoanalysis, couples therapy, and family therapy alike, stands as an essential resource for therapists of all stripes.

Mary-Joan Gerson PHD ABPP

Mary-Joan Gerson

New York, NY 10019, USA
mjgerson@gmail.com

Mary-Joan Gerson   Professor and Supervisor at the N.Y.U. Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis and directed the Advanced Specialization in Couple and Family Therapy at that Institute. She is the founding president of Section VIII, Couples and Family Therapy, of Division 39, APA, and the author of The Embedded Self, Second Edition: An Integrative Psychodynamic and Systemic Perspective on Couples and Family Therapy (2009). New York: Taylor and Francis (Routledge).

Reading, again, Romantic Bonds, Binds, and Ruptures: Couples on the Brink evoked in me new admiration for Virginia Goldner’s comprehensive review of the scope of couples therapy. Goldner makes clear that when treating couples on the brink, and I think any couple, this work is significantly different from dyadic psychoanalytic treatment. She emphasizes that the intense engagement in the room is between the partners, however powerful her own internal response. Recognition of the conjoining of partners can actually relieve shame and deepen attachment. Most helpfully, Goldner discusses the fears and failures of adult attachment and articulates the thrill of repair after rupture, as a profound experience in intimate relationships.

Disorganized Attachment in Romantic Couples: A Cross-Cultural Relational Approach (USA–Mexico)

Marcela López González PHD

Marcela López González

Benito Fernández Arrieta #62 Casa 1, Col. Los Cipreses, Alcaldía Coyoacán, Ciudad de México C.P. 04830, México
marcela.iarpp.mx@gmail.com

Marcela López González   The author of: “The importance of frustration and tolerance to frustration in the Psychoanalytic clinic” and “The Destructuring Mexican Family: its complex psychodynamics.” As a postgraduate student of Latin American Psychoanalysis, she is working toward diplomas in Relational Psychoanalysis and contemporary techniques. Her private practice is in Mexico City where she works with adults, adolescents, couples, and families.

This brief reflection on Virginia Goldner’s essay, Romantic ties, Attachments, and Ruptures: Couples on the Brink, places the debate on disorganized attachment in couples within a cross-cultural context and examines how dynamics at the individual and group level are likely to influence couples who seek treatment having reached an impasse in their relationship. In the article “Bonds, Attachments, and Romantic Breakups: Couples on the Edge of the Abyss,” Virginia Goldner presents a complex analysis of the elements involved in the Relational Psychoanalytic Approach to Couples Therapy, particularly in romantic relationships that reach an impasse in conflict resolution. Her work is valuable for deeper exploration and practical application in clinical settings. Although it is developed within a specific cultural and societal context – where, in the U.S. the population tends to be more individualistic, young people often leave home to live independently, and affective bonds are generally less tight, facilitating more practical separations and divorces – these cultural patterns contrast with those in Mexico. In Mexico, children tend to have stronger ties with their families, leave the household later, and are influenced by predominant Catholic values, which often delay decisions regarding separation or divorce. Additionally, because individuals are more emotionally enmeshed with their families, relationship dynamics become more complex, with family members often playing a significant role in couples’ conflicts.

Esta breve reflexión sobre el ensayo de Virginia Goldner, Lazos románticos, apegos y rupturas: parejas al borde del abismo, sitúa el debate sobre el apego desorganizado en las parejas en un contexto transcultural y examina cómo las dinámicas a nivel individual y grupal pueden influir en las parejas que buscan tratamiento tras haber llegado a un punto muerto en su relación.
