Listening from the Brink

Leigh Lyndon Psy.D.

Leigh Lyndon

San Francisco, California, USA
5665 College Avenue Suite 220C, Oakland, CA 94618, USA
leigh@drleighlyndon.com

Leigh Lyndon   Clinical psychologist and psychoanalyst who maintains a private practice in Oakland. She has taught and supervised at Access Institute, California Pacific Medical Center, and The Psychotherapy Institute. She also co-facilitates a case conference for Peer Counselors at RAMS in San Francisco as part of her ongoing work with the Community Psychoanalytic Track at Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California where she is also the Dean of Candidates. In an earlier chapter of her life, she was a choreographer and performing artist in New York City.

In my response to Goldner’s 2014 paper, I link her work with couples on the brink to the current socio-enviro-political scene. With the climate catastrophe unfolding at a rapid pace, the political divisions are becoming ever more entrenched and dangerous. Is this a defense against the unimaginable? In this prose poem, I hope to draw the reader into a state in which they can tune into their own experience of living in a world on the brink.

responseSet.title: This Love Business: Attending to the Embodiment of Both the Therapist and Both Partners in Couples Therapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

This Love Business: Attending to the Embodiment of Both the Therapist and Both Partners in Couples Therapy

Stacy Malin PHD

Stacy Malin

New York, New York, USA
stacy.malin@gmail.com

Stacy Malin   A psychologist and graduate of the NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, Dr. Malin is also a devoted couples therapist. She has pursued multiple trainings in couples therapy, including a Psychobiological Approach to Couples Therapy (PACT), Emotion-Focused Therapy (EFT), Relational Life Therapy (RLT), and other modalities, and has presented widely on the topic. In addition, Dr. Malin is a somatic experiencing practitioner (SEP) and attachment-based EMDR therapist and she integrates both trauma-based approaches in individual and couples therapy. Dr. Malin is on the faculty of both the Stephen Mitchell Relational Study Center and the Couples Therapy Training and Program at the William Alanson White Institute. She serves as clinical consultant to the Philadelphia Couples Therapy (PCT) program, maintains a private practice in NYC, and runs supervision groups in couples therapy.

Revisiting Virginia Goldner’s brilliant, lyrical article “Romantic Bonds, Binds and Ruptures,” this author advocates for the integration of a somatic approach in working with couples on the brink. In addition, the impact of the romantic life of the couples therapist on the therapist’s embodied presence and subjectivity is brought into question; in particular, the author speculates that the couples therapist’s own romantic wounds may serve either as an impediment or alternatively as a source of heightened emotional attunement in couples therapy.

responseSet.title: Love is Louder
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Love is Louder

Jean-Emmanuel Malpas L.M.F.T.-D. L.M.H.C.-D.

Jean-Emmanuel Malpas

jean@jeanmalpas.com

Jean-Emmanuel Malpas   Trained in clinical psychology at Brussels University, Belgium. He is the co-founder of the Couple & Intimacy Project at the Ackerman Institute for the Family. He is a NYS licensed marriage and family therapist and mental health counselor. He maintains a private practice, JEM Therapy, in NYC.

Reflecting on Goldner’s iconic “Romantic Bonds, Binds, and Ruptures: Couples on the Brink,” Malpas highlights the importance of intra-individual and interpersonal changes in couple therapy. Anchoring this predilection for a both/and approach in personal experience and clinical training, he reviews complementary models as entry points into the emotional, relational, and systemic complexity of the love bond. Connecting the personal to the political, Malpas wonders about the humanistic value of relational work.

Virginia Goldner’s Seminal Contributions to Psychoanalytic Couple Psychotherapy

Shelley Nathans PHD

Shelley Nathans

San Francisco, California, USA
5625 College Avenue #213, Oakland, CA 94618

Shelley Nathans   The faculties of The Psychoanalytic Couple Psychotherapy Group, The Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California, and The San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis. She is the director/producer of the film, Robert Wallerstein: 65 Years at the Center of Psychoanalysis and is on the international board of Couple and Family Psychoanalysis. Her publications include, Infidelity as Manic Defence (2015), published in Couple and Family Psychoanalysis, “45 Years: an essay on the film by Andrew Haigh, published in fort da (2018); “Whose Disgust is it Anyway?: Projection and Projective Identification in the Couple Relationship, in Psychoanalytic Dialogues (2016); and. “Oedipus for Everyone: Revitalizing the Model for LBGTQ Couples and Single Parent Families,’’ in Psychoanalytic Dialogues (2021). She is co-editor, with Milton Schaefer, of Couples on the Couch: Psychoanalytic Couple Psychotherapy and the Tavistock Model (Routledge, 2017) and the editor of a second volume of collected papers about psychoanalytic couple psychotherapy entitled, More About Couples on the Couch: Approaching Psychoanalytic Couple Psychotherapy from an Expanded Perspective (2022). Dr. Nathans is in private practice in Oakland, California.

Virginia Goldner’s paper, Romantic Bonds, Binds, and Ruptures (2014) is discussed in terms of her important contributions to psychoanalytic couple theory and technique. I contextualize this paper with reference to her earlier, innovative work treating couples presenting with domestic violence. I emphasize the value of her integration of the social unconscious, feminist theory, systems theory, and relational psychoanalytic theory in her approach to understanding the mental processes and relentless enactments common to many couple relationships.

responseSet.title: Sharin’ the Love of So Many Ideas
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sharin’ the Love of So Many Ideas

Philip A. Ringstrom PSYD PHD

Philip A. Ringstrom

USA
15527 Valley Vista Blvd, Encino, Los Angeles, CA 91436
Ringsite@aol.com

Philip A. Ringstrom   Senior Training and Supervising Analyst and Faculty Member at the Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, in Los Angeles, California. He is a founding member of the Board of Directors of IARPP, the International Association of Relational Psychotherapists and Psychoanalysts, and a member of the International Council of Self-Psychologists. He is on the editorial boards of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, Psychoanalytic Perspectives, and Psychoanalysis: Self and Context. His book A Relational Psychoanalytic Approach to Conjoint Therapy (Routledge 2014) won the Goethe Award for Psychoanalytic and Psychodynamic Scholarship in Psychoanalysis for 2014. He is currently writing a new book that will be titled Psychoanalytic Play: Dramatization, Narration, and Improvisation in Field Theory and Metapsychology.

This brief article reflects my shared enthusiasm of Virginia Goldner’s love of so many ideas similar to my own in my book A Relational Psychoanalytic Approachto Couples Psychotherapy, Routledge, 2014. In this commentary, I frame my comments through the lens of my book’s relational psychoanalytic approach. Its organization is based on three essential themes, all of which are articulated throughout “six steps,” with each step falling under a conceptual arch involving both symmetrical and asymmetrical approaches to psychotherapy. I close with an example of how I might engage Goldner’s patients “Bill and Jane” in disarming their battle to the death in therapy, involving speaking to them as if I embodied what my third theme refers to as the couple’s “relationship having a ‘mind’ of its own.”

responseSet.title: Thinking About Couples on the Brink in a World that is Also on the Brink
responseSet.authorMast: Ellen F. Wachtel and  Paul L. Wachtel
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thinking About Couples on the Brink in a World that is Also on the Brink

Ellen F. Wachtel J.D. PHD and Paul L. Wachtel PHD

Paul L. Wachtel

New York, New York, USA

New York, NY 10031, USA
paul.wachtel@gmail.com

Paul L. Wachtel   Distinguished Professor of psychology in the doctoral program in clinical psychology at the City College of New York. Among his books are Psychoanalysis, Behavior Therapy, and the Relational World; Family Dynamics in Individual Psychotherapy (with Ellen Wachtel); Race in the Mind of America; Relational Theory and the Practice of Psychotherapy; Therapeutic Communication; Inside the Session; The Poverty of Affluence; and Cyclical Psychodynamics and the Contextual Self. His most recent book is Making Room for the Disavowed: Reclaiming the Self in Psychotherapy. He was awarded the Hans H. Strupp Award for Psychoanalytic Writing, Teaching, and Research; the Distinguished Psychologist Award by Division 29 (Psychotherapy) of APA; the Scholarship and Research Award by Division 39 (Psychoanalysis) of APA; and the Sidney J. Blatt Award for Outstanding Contributions to Psychotherapy, Scholarship, Education and Practice.

This paper presents commentary on Virginia Goldner’s 2014 paper, Romantic Bonds, Binds, and Ruptures: Couples on the Brink. It considers the ways in which ideas that derive from couple work and from psychoanalytic theory apply to a larger set of challenges facing the world today. One central focus is on the experience of victimhood and the difficult to resolve dynamics that ensue when both sides frame their impasse as almost exclusively the product of the other’s behavior. We discuss as well some features of couple work that differ from individual therapy and how that modifies the way the work needs to be structured and pursued.

responseSet.title: Reflection, Representation, and Diffraction in the Analytic Process Based Upon Quantum Theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reflection, Representation, and Diffraction in the Analytic Process Based Upon Quantum Theory

Deborah Bryon PHD

Deborah Bryon

Caminho da Fonte, nº 42, parish of São Gonçalo, Madeira 9060-112, Portugal
deborahbryon@gmail.com

Deborah Bryon   Member of International Association of Jungian Analysts (IAAP) and Inter-Regional Society of Jungian Analysts (IRSJA). She is in private practice and the author of three books Lessons of the Inca shamans, Part I: Piercing the veil (Pine Winds Press, 2012), Lessons of the Inca shamans, Part II: Beyond the veil (Pine Winds Press, 2014), and Time and Trauma in Analytical Psychology and Psychotherapy: The Wisdom of Andean shamanism (Routledge, 2023) is in press. Her most recent publications include “Implicit states of connectivity in the clinical practice of Jungian psychoanalysis and Andean shamanism” (Journal of Analytical Psychology, 2023), “Are Psychedelics Really Necessary?” in Psychedelics and Individuation, ed Les Stein and Lionel Corbett (2023), “Bastard children of the avant-garde: artistic expression in the cubist and abstract expressionist movements and the psychoanalytic process” (Journal of Analytical Psychology, May 2022), and “Processing trauma in psychoanalysis in real time and in dreams: The convergence of past, present, and future during covid 19” (Journal of Analytic Psychology, 2021).

An overview of the fundamental concepts that grew out of quantum physics, which created a new systemic structural approach in relational psychoanalysis corresponding with fractal geometry and aspects of depth and other psychologies will be presented, applied to dynamic ways of engaging occur within the analytic encounter. Reflection, representation, and diffraction are among three forms of interaction that happen in quantum physics that physicist Karen Barad has described, also occurring within the transference dynamic. These forms of engagement will be explored from both an intersubjective, postmodern view, and from a fractal epistemology perspective, which includes potential for a fixed observer dependence based upon a phenomenological distinction between objects and the ways they are perceived and experienced, influenced by both position and scale Marks-Tarlow and Shapiro, An application of anonymous case material will be included that illustrates the concepts being presented from a relational perspective related to diffraction and fractal dynamism theories based upon quantum principles proposed by Bohm. An objective of this paper is to synthesize a conceptualization of psychoanalytic theory primarily from a relational focus, with quantum theory and fractal dynamics woven together, in an integration process related to diffraction, representational, and reflective processes.

responseSet.title: Why Physics? Commentary on Reflection, Representation, and Diffraction in the Analytic Process
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Why Physics? Commentary on Reflection, Representation, and Diffraction in the Analytic Process

Terry Marks-Tarlow PHD

Terry Marks-Tarlow

1460 7th Street, Suite 304, Santa Monica, CA 90401, USA
markstarlow@hotmail.com

Terry Marks-Tarlow   Clinical psychologist in private practice in Santa Monica, California, who teaches and trains through the Insight Center, Los Angeles, Pacifica Graduate Institute, Santa Barbara, California, and California Institute of Integral Studies, San Francisco, California.

In her paper on “Reflection, Representation, and Diffraction in the Analytic Process,” Deborah Bryon integrates the work of physicists Karen Barad and David Bohm with nonlinear dynamics under the framework of a fractal epistemology. She examines the relevance of three forms of interaction within physics – reflection, representation, and diffraction – to the psychoanalytic dyad. By so doing, she adds complexity and nuance to the terrains of theorizing in relational psychoanalysis. This commentary supports the significance of Bryon’s paper by contextualizing it historically. Sections to follow examine the role that scientific metaphors and models play in the evolution of psychoanalytic theory. Physics is an especially good match because it involves the exploration of hidden, often invisible dimensions related to patterns in space, time, as well as atemporal expanses.

responseSet.newSectionName: Notes from the Field: Therapists Working in Wartime Ukraine
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction

Amy Cooney PHD

Amy Cooney

New York, New York, USA

Amy Cooney  

This special panel moves beyond the consulting room and into the field, presenting a compelling series of essays by candidates at the Ukrainian School of Relational Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy in Kyiv, alongside contributions from the institute’s founder, Alyona Chukanova, and translator Oksana Lyzak. This collection captures the personal and clinical reflections of these remarkable therapists, who navigate the dual role of analytic trainees and therapists amidst the trauma of war.

These essays offer a visceral portrayal of the psychological landscape of wartime Ukraine, illuminating experiences of fear, dread, guilt, shame, and rage, as well as resilience, hope, strength, and love. We hear from clinicians who, alongside their patients, endure nightly drone strikes, rolling power outages, profound uncertainty, and unbearable loss. Their narratives depict their efforts to survive emotionally as individuals and as therapists.

Framing this series are commentaries by New York-based supervisors Amy Schwartz Cooney and Rachel Sopher, who serve as witnesses and companions from afar, engaging in virtual dialogues with their Ukrainian colleagues. Their reflections capture the dizzying juxtapositions inherent in this reality: the extraordinary interwoven with the mundane, the terrifying coexisting with the reassuring, the incomprehensible unfolding alongside the familiar.

This special panel affirms the life-sustaining power of relational connection, underscoring the profound importance of community and therapeutic engagement across geographical, temporal, and existential divides. We are honored to share these voices from Ukraine.

Amy Schwartz Cooney, Ph.D.

New York, New York, USA

259

responseSet.title: Consulting from the Heart
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Consulting from the Heart

Amy Cooney PHD

Amy Cooney

New York, New York, USA

Amy Cooney  

Founded just before the war (or “full scale invasion” as it is known) by Alyona Chukanova, the Ukrainian Institute is an international training program in contemporary relational theory and clinical technique. Alyona, an emotional and intellectual power-house, has developed a comprehensive curriculum introducing trainees to two-person psychologies and has invited instructors from around the world to teach remotely. Beyond didactic training, Alyona has recruited supervisors to conduct biweekly three-hour consultation sessions, facilitated through translation.

Eighteen months ago, Alyona invited me to join as a consultant for the current cohort. The supervisory sessions, conducted over Zoom, are structured with two trainees presenting for 90 minutes each. A candidate speaks briefly, followed by our translator, Oksana – an MD, PhD, whose husband is a medical doctor on the eastern front – who translates into English. I respond as best I can, and Oksana translates back into Ukrainian.

The process of working with this group can feel surreal – at times, like a comedy of errors. For example, I might ask, “How did you feel when your patient described his complex emotions about his wife?” The candidate responds at length, their hands gesturing expressively, as Zoom emojis flicker across the screen and the group reacts with sympathy, outrage, or understanding. When I turn to Oksana for translation, she laconically reports, looking up from her extensive notes, “I felt good.” I exclaim, “That’s it?!” The group bursts into laughter, recognizing both the gaps in translation and the unexpected discoveries. These moments of disjunction paradoxically draw us closer, reinforcing our shared connection.

Through my work with these candidates I have glimpsed their lives and those of their patients: conducting sessions as the light dims from electrical outages, worrying about colleagues in Odessa after nights of heavy bombing, and feeling relief when a delayed response turns out to be due to a babysitter’s lateness rather than tragedy.

responseSet.title: Reflections on the Psychological and Relational Impact of War
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reflections on the Psychological and Relational Impact of War

Alyona Esse-Chukanova MA

Alyona Esse-Chukanova

Lviv, Ukraine

Alyona Esse-Chukanova  

By the time this publication is released three years will have passed since the full-scale invasion of Ukraine.

I vividly remember what I wrote during the first year of the invasion in another journal (Psychoanalysis, Self and Context, 18(3), 2023). Today, I wouldn’t change much of it. On the contrary, three years later, my understanding of what I expressed back then has deepened. Perhaps in 10 years, the stories of how I experienced this war will make more sense than they do now. At present, I struggle to form a coherent narrative, a reflection of the fractured world we live in today. Despite lacking answers, I continue to ask questions.

The war has fundamentally transformed our lives – our work, relationships, and our basic ways of existing. We’ve learned to adapt to war: to live, protect, love, work, and even vacation amidst it. But at what cost?

In therapy sessions, patients rarely speak directly about the war. Instead, they discuss relationships, daily challenges, and work. While the war is omnipresent in our reality, it’s rarely the overt subject of discussion. We all know it’s there, that we’re all in danger. Are we frozen? Is this paralysis our response to the ongoing crime, to the unanswered question of when the killing will stop?

As a therapist, I’ve noticed that I listen more and comment less in sessions now. Have I regained my sense of asymmetry? Have I reclaimed the responsibility that comes with my role?

Our training continues. We study and practice the Relational Psychoanalysis. But I wonder – are we succeeding? Is it even possible in these times?

Over the past three years, we’ve learned much about ourselves – both good and bad. But is a dialogue within ourselves or with others truly possible? What kind of people will we be when the war ends?

We all share one question: when will the war end? This is not just our question; it’s a question for the world, which is also grappling with its own crises and lacks answers.

Perhaps this is our new reality – not just for Ukraine but for the entire world. Will we need to change our theories and frameworks to address this prolonged state of war?

responseSet.title: A Story with No Ending
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Story with No Ending

Oksana Lyzak MD PHD

Oksana Lyzak

Lviv, Ukraine

Oksana Lyzak  

Introduction

War is a relentless force that leaves no aspect of life untouched, weaving itself into the fabric of daily existence and reshaping even the most intimate connections. This story, written from the perspective of a mother, a wife, and a professional, seeks to capture the enduring effects of war on relationships, identity, and resilience.

2021: Prelude

“Froggy, I’m thinking of getting an AR-15. I want to learn how to shoot.”

“I thought hunters knew how to shoot.”

“That’s different. I need to learn how to shoot in warfare.”

“Come on. They won’t dare.”

February 24th, 2022: The First Day

The day began with a summons. My husband, part of the territorial defense forces, became a company commander. I was left with our three children, glued to the news, unable to leave the house.

The Weight of Resentment

During those first weeks, my emotions spiraled between resentment, anger and despair. The injustice felt suffocating. My husband, a father of three, was not supposed to be sent to war. Why him?The days were a blur of rage and survival. The uncertainty of his safety permeated every waking thought.

A Constant State of Waiting

Every day began with checking his messages: “Good morning, everyone, everything’s fine.” A short, reassuring phrase that became a lifeline. But the thoughts came anyway. What if he’s injured? What if he loses a limb? His eyesight? His mind?

The First Death

In autumn, the war claimed its first victim from his company. Standing at the military burial field during the funeral, I couldn’t help but think: “I don’t want my husband to lie here. I want my grave to be next to his.

264

responseSet.title: The Complexity of Experiencing War from a Distance
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Complexity of Experiencing War from a Distance

Iryna Blazhina PHD

Iryna Blazhina

Chernivtsi, Ukraine

Iryna Blazhina  

In late January 2024, a patient, N., sought help due to her inability to cope with intense emotions triggered by her husband’s relationship with his adult son from a previous marriage. The son, who lives separately, suffers from a mental illness and drug addiction. The father refuses him nothing and treats him like a child, fulfilling his every whim.

N. moved abroad ten years ago, secured a job, and remarried four years ago. From her first marriage, she has two daughters: the elder one lives and works in another country, while the younger one moved from Ukraine to live with her mother after the full-scale invasion began. N. had offered other family members the opportunity to relocate, but they declined. Throughout this time, she had been deeply worried about them and provided financial support.

Additionally, she donated more than half of her earnings to charity, aiding Ukrainians affected by the war. It was as though she was atoning for the guilt of not being in Ukraine, unable to fully share in her loved ones’ suffering, and for having a relatively comfortable life, free to travel and live without bombings or the restrictions her family endured.

During psychotherapy, it became evident that N.’s mother treated her older brother in a manner similar to how her husband treated his son. Her mother had to send her son to her parents when she was about to give birth to N. and kept him there until N. was a year old. Since then, her mother had favored the elder son, indulging him and treating him better than her daughter. Currently, he lives alone with their mother and struggles with alcoholism. I believe that N’s first marriage was an unconscious attempt to escape from home.

Throughout the war, N. closely followed the news of military events and experienced intense distress. In November 2024, after yet another attack on her hometown, she woke up in the middle of the night and realized she could no longer endure it. It was time to let go of her intense involvement with the events in Ukraine and their impact in an effort to preserve herself. She asked her acquaintances to stop expressing sympathy and refrained from inquiring about her family’s well-being after each new attack as she felt unable to intervene in any way or convince her loved ones to join her in safety.

responseSet.title: Changes in Group Therapy Dynamics During War
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Changes in Group Therapy Dynamics During War

Iryna Blazhina PHD

Iryna Blazhina

Chernivtsi, Ukraine

Iryna Blazhina  

The group started its work in September 2024. Setting: once a week, 1.5-hour sessions. The group consisted of eight participants (three men and five women) and two female psychotherapists. The participants’ ages ranged from 27 to 45. Most of them were from Chernivtsi, with one participant from the Kyiv region and another from Kharkiv. The latter two had relocated to Chernivtsi in March 2022 due to the full-scale invasion. All participants had undergone individual psychotherapy for varying durations (from 1.5 to 7 years).

At the beginning, there were many expressions of fear regarding self-disclosure within the group, as well as resentment toward the psychotherapists who had brought them there. Some participants voiced uncertainty about whether group therapy suited them, expressing hesitation about staying long-term and struggling to articulate their thoughts. By the third or fourth session, comments such as, ‘I don’t understand anything, but it’s very interesting,’ started emerging. Participants with prior psychotherapy experience were more active and openly discussed personal issues.

During the fourth session, one of the psychotherapists was absent. A participant shared a dream in which she was walking through a forest and saw the therapist approaching her on a suspension bridge. Behind the therapist, a shaggy monster was chasing her. The participant was waving her hands, urging the therapist to hurry and not look back. She interpreted the monster as the second psychotherapist. This led to a broader discussion in the group about the desire for individual therapy.

In the second month, a complaint was raised against a more ‘experienced’ participant for ‘invalidating’ another participant’s story in the previous session. He was surprised, as he had not realized this. The participant requested that he be more sensitive to her. In the next session, the same participant seemingly inadvertently referred to the group leaders as ‘coaches,’ which surprised the therapists. This was also interpreted as a form of devaluation.

responseSet.title: Constructive Profession in Destructive Times
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Constructive Profession in Destructive Times

Olena Khrystych MA

Olena Khrystych

Kyiv, Ukraine

Olena Khrystych  

War is not only about aggression and destruction; it is also about death. Facing death is terrifying for most people, including myself. My first confrontation with the reality of death occurred at the age of seven. It was my 10th day of school when I learned that my father had died in a car accident abroad. Days passed before his body, enclosed in a sealed coffin, was transported home. This traumatic event shattered my childhood illusion of safety and instilled in me a profound awareness of life’s fragility.

Now, living in the midst of the full-scale invasion, uncertainty and danger have escalated to levels not experienced since World War II. War demolishes lives and destabilizes the illusion of goodness and stability in the world. As Einstein wrote to Freud in 1932, war is a “collective psychosis,” revealing a destructive drive that resides within humans, usually hidden but unleashed in extraordinary circumstances and manifested in horror and destruction.

I cannot stop the war; how can I endure it? My initial efforts to cope involved turning to family, friends, and hobbies — tangible, external sources of comfort. Yet, as a psychoanalyst, I knew I must find deeper resources within myself. Reflecting on my experiences, I recognized the strength of my internalized object: my mother. Despite losing her husband unexpectedly, she remained composed and reliable, providing my brother and me with a sense of safety amidst external chaos throughout our lives. This internalized stability, this container forged through my mother’s fortitude and love, now serves as a source of strength in my work with patients.

Another coping strategy during these terrible times lies in accepting reality and allowing myself to feel whatever arises without denial. In times of crisis as a psychoanalyst, I find myself working more intersubjectivly, emphasizing shared experiences and mutual influence within the therapeutic relationship as my patients and I co-exist in states of shared trauma and external threat.

responseSet.title: Emigration (Refugee-Hood) During War: Personal and Analytic Reflections
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Emigration (Refugee-Hood) During War: Personal and Analytic Reflections

Nina Kryzhanivska MA

Nina Kryzhanivska

Kyiv, Ukraine

Nina Kryzhanivska  

Emigration (refugee-hood) during war is a painful experience that presents individuals with new challenges and questions that once seemed distant and irrelevant. It affects not only the loss of physical space but also shatters the connections that defined our identity. The war forced me to leave my home, my country, and my familiar life. However, the greatest loss was the sense of internal stability – it felt as though the ground beneath me had been swept away, compelling me to confront what I thought was long buried in familial memory.

When I left Ukraine, my thoughts and feelings were contradictory. On the one hand, I chose to leave for safety’s sake. On the other hand, every step toward the border felt like a betrayal – a betrayal of the land that raised me, the people who stayed behind, and even the part of myself that still clung to the familiarity of home despite its dangers.

The loss of home made me question: What exactly do I call home? Is it a physical space? The relationships that shaped me as a person? Or is it merely the illusion of stability that was always fragile? Each day seemed to heighten these questions and the loss I felt. The most painful part was seeing how it affected my adult daughter. Her world, like mine, was destroyed. Yet, in her eyes, I saw not just despair but also hope – hope for the future, for rebuilding, and for the possibility of creating something new.

At that moment, I realized that my duty as a mother was not only to protect her physically but also to help her preserve faith in herself and in the possibility of rebuilding, even from fragments.

Fleeing my country also became a journey into the depths of my family history. I realized that this experience was not new. My family had endured similar trials, as each generation had been forced to leave their homes due to wars, revolutions, or other disasters. I felt the same fears and sensations that likely accompanied my ancestors. Like them, I wanted to protect my child but felt the weight of responsibility was overwhelming because no action I could take would change the circumstances. In this intergenerational repetition, I saw a kind of fatalism, as though fleeing was ingrained in our DNA. Yet, it also prompted me to pause and think: How can this cycle be broken? How can we create something that isn’t solely defined by flight and trauma?

responseSet.title: Subjectivity in Therapy During War
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Subjectivity in Therapy During War

Olga Mukha MD

Olga Mukha

Vinnytsia, Ukraine

Olga Mukha  

Twenty years ago despite a general trend among psychiatrists toward psychoanalysis, I chose person-centered therapy because I found psychoanalysis lacking sensitivity, empathy, and relational engagement which I considered essential in those earlier days.

Stephen Mitchell (1977) argued that the analyst’s subjectivity is always present in therapy. Our internal dynamics interact with those of our patients, shaping the therapeutic process. Therapists are never invisible in the therapeutic space, no matter how much we may strive toward neutrality and anonymity. Russia's full-scale war against Ukraine has compelled us to be more open with our patients, as we share common traumatic experience. At times, however, the therapist's self-disclosure can introduce unbearable tension into the therapeutic space.

One young woman started therapy a year before the full-scale war to cope with her intense emotions and her profound fear of traveling alone. Professionally successful, she struggled with low self-esteem and had no romantic relationships. Food and art served as her primary sources of sensory gratification. Her father’s cruelty had left her hypervigilant to anger, and the time limitations of a 50-minute session often provoked resentment. The therapy continued, and then came the morning of February 24. Thus, the question arises: How is the therapist's subjectivity present in the therapeutic space during the war when you are a therapist from Ukraine and your patient is a Russian woman from Moscow?

That morning, I informed my patient of Russia’s attack on Ukraine. This openness shifted the therapeutic dynamic, creating a new space of shared vulnerability. She expressed empathy for my pain and fear, perhaps connecting it to her own experience of being attacked by her abusive father. Yet, her nationality introduced unspoken tensions into our therapeutic relationships.

While I expressed no anger toward her personally, the broader political and emotional context complicated our sessions. Her memories of her father’s abuse resonated deeply with me, evoking parallels that were impossible to ignore. Therapy became a space for her to explore feelings of guilt and a need for forgiveness, while I wrestled with my own ambivalence and inner conflicts. Over time, she began to explore new possibilities, such as travelling independently. Her joy in discovering safety and comfort abroad brought mixed emotions. While I was pleased for her growth as an individual, I found it difficult to reconcile this with the ongoing destructions and sufferings in Ukraine, where I had chosen to remain.

responseSet.title: The Therapeutic Relationships Amid Catastrophic Disruption of Life Ties
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Therapeutic Relationships Amid Catastrophic Disruption of Life Ties

Nataliia Secretariuk MD

Nataliia Secretariuk

Kyiv, Ukraine

Nataliia Secretariuk  

In this paper, I share my personal experience of how the therapist’s state affects therapeutic relationships during the catastrophic disruption of life ties caused by the war and their partial restoration.

As a psychoanalytically oriented therapist working with adults, and as a mother of two daughters and grandmother of three grandchildren, the war has profoundly shaped my professional and personal worlds.

My first scheduled session after the invasion was with a young woman living in another country, who struggled deeply with feelings of helplessness. On that day, I felt I needed to remain a stable and good object for her, despite my own shaken reality.

The night before, explosions shook my home at 4 a.m., reducing the protective walls of my apartment to mere cardboard in my mind. Contemplating my options, I realized that survival would mean leaving everything behind – memories, beloved objects, and comfort. I gathered essential items, including my phone, computer, and three children’s books I had planned to give my grandchildren and left to find shelter with relatives.

The next day, I returned home, feeling like a stranger. I opened my laptop and sat in the familiar chair where I always worked, trying to pretend nothing had changed. Yet, my unease lingered. I messaged my client, letting her know I was ready for the session and acknowledging that I had been running late due to interference. Her response was immediate: “I won’t be there. I already have other plans. You didn’t warn me.”

The pain of this exchange was unbearable, plunging me into a void of emptiness and loneliness. Over time, I was able to process my feelings of betrayal, helplessness, and despair.

What saved me from complete disconnection was the formation of new ties. Returning to my relative’s home, I found myself in a lively, nurturing environment filled with conversation, shared meals, and laughter. This vibrant atmosphere provided me with relief.

Soon, psychological support groups emerged, offering an extraordinary sense of solidarity and care. For the first time in my life, I experienced support of such depth and sincerity. It came from colleagues, friends, and even strangers, who enveloped me with care, attention, and respect, slowly pulling me out of the void.

responseSet.title: Relationality as a New Formation of War in the Therapeutic Process
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Relationality as a New Formation of War in the Therapeutic Process

Kateryna Shelest MA

Kateryna Shelest

Dnipro, Ukraine

Kateryna Shelest  

In this article, I reflect on how the war has impacted the therapeutic process and how it has led me toward true relationality in my practice. The therapeutic relationship in the context of war inevitably requires the therapist to bring their subjectivity into the process and to work in the context of shared vulnerability. The war struck like a tornado – not only in my country but also in my personal life and identity. Suddenly, I no longer knew what was important. I recall how, in the first months after the war began, I awoke every day weighing various factors, the significance of which for me then was no longer so clear to me at that time. The only way forward was to listen to my body, emotions, and perceptions, gathering myself piece by piece: What do I want now? What is important in these new conditions? What do I hold on to, and what must I leave behind? Packing an “emergency suitcase” became a metaphor for this existential audit. It wasn’t just about physical things; it felt like I had to decide what to carry into my future and what to leave behind. Every day I grappled with this relentless internal work, defining meanings and determining priorities under conditions where life itself felt precarious. Exhaustion became a constant companion. Despite the dangers of nightly shelling, sleep was a relief – a momentary reprieve from ruminating on decisions and choices that could determine life or death.

In this fragmented state, I returned to the therapeutic process just weeks after the war began. Together with my clients, we restored the structure and therapeutic setting. However, this was a new and profound vulnerability. My previous therapeutic stance, which had generally been more neutral, now had to accommodate a shared traumatic reality. For the first time, neither my clients nor I had the words to describe our experiences (Stern, 1997).

Therapeutic work was both transformed and transformative. The one stable and reliable element became the relational space we were continually creating. Long silences became frequent during sessions. I remember how hard it was to endure this silence, as the pain, terror, despair and hopelessness felt so loud. I wanted to find words to name what was happening, but all that came to mind could not fully describe this experience. These were filled not with words but with sensations – heavy breathing, chest tightness, and constriction in the throat (Fonagy et al., 2002). Attempts to describe these experiences in words often felt inadequate; only visual images and fantasies could fully encompass the magnitude of our sensations (Bucci, 2002). We created meaning from chaos, using relational space as a transformative enviromant (Winnicott, 1967). The therapeutic space morphed to accommodate the ever-changing realities: internal, external, and relational.

responseSet.title: Good and Evil in the Psychotherapy Room During Wartime
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Good and Evil in the Psychotherapy Room During Wartime

Olga Stepashko BA

Olga Stepashko

Kyiv, Ukraine

Olga Stepashko  

War, here and now in one’s own country, becomes an all-encompassing experience for both the individual and society. The experience of the self, the coherence of self, and social connections take on heightened forms.

War amplifies and, at the same time, obscures the perception of both reality and subjectivity. Stressful events significantly influence the interplay and affective coloring of our various dissociated parts of self.

Ukraine has become the victim of an unjust and brutal attack by a state possessing nuclear weapons. At the same time, Ukraine has mounted a dignified, years-long resistance against the aggressor. The threat to an entire nation’s existence and prolonged personal danger coexists with pride, resistance, and hope – both on a social and personal level. The complexity of reconciling such experiences undoubtedly contributes to heightened internal psychic conflict and dissociations. On the other hand, contemporary Ukrainians are increasingly able to draw parallels between military and political events and better comprehend their paradoxical states – their helplessness and agency – as being more interconnected than they once seemed.

Can we determine whether some suffer more in this war than others? Can we think in such terms at all? Although good and evil have become more sharply defined for Ukrainians, the range of experiences among individuals is markedly diverse. This diversity exists not only between different people, families, and groups of Ukrainians but also within the experiences of a single individual, which can become extreme. At the same time, we observe how this very process of “splitting the world” into black and white, into a realm of safety and a realm of threat, into enemies and allies, fosters both division and unity among people – thanks, in part, to dissociation. This phenomenon extends to the dynamics between patient and therapist, intensifying both connection and separation.

I will illustrate these reflections with three vignettes. Each of these women began psychoanalytic therapy long before the full-scale invasion. None have experienced the loss of loved ones or property due to the war, and all are able to continue their psychoanalytic treatment. Yet, the themes of good and bad, right and wrong, guilt and gratitude, shame and pride, safety and horror have sharpened, leading to new discoveries.

responseSet.title: “Navigating through the Storm”: A Shelter Among Shadows
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Navigating through the Storm”: A Shelter Among Shadows

Theodoros Tsirigotis PHD

Theodoros Tsirigotis

Chania, Crete, Greece

Theodoros Tsirigotis  

During 2023-2024, I found myself in a unique and deeply transformative position: Serving as psychotherapist in a training group composed entirely of Ukrainian colleagues who were navigating the realities of war. This experience offered a profound intersection of professional growth and personal empathy, as I adapted my role among colleagues whose lives were directly shaped by the ongoing war in Ukraine. Reflecting on this journey, I aim to share how it reshaped my perspectives on psychological practice, cross-cultural connection, and resilience.

The training group was designed to foster cross-cultural dialogue and professional development among psychologists from diverse backgrounds. However, in this setting, “diversity” took on a nuanced meaning – rooted in a shared profession but profoundly marked by differences in lived realities.

My Ukrainian colleagues brought narrative of strength, loss, and endurance, intricately tied to their nation’s battle for survival. Their contributions transformed the group into a mosaic of resilience and struggle. Stepping into this environment, I became acutely aware of my unique position as an outsider – not just geographically but experientially. The group evolved into a space where personal stories intertwine with the broader context of war, offering unparalleled insights into the psychological dimensions of conflict.

Being the sole participant from outside the conflict zone presented complex emotional and professional challenges. From the outset, I was acutely aware of my unique position Winnicott (1965)—not just geographically but experientially. I grappled with an underlying sense of survivor's guilt, a relational dynamic often explored in psychoanalytic thought, particularly through the lens of intersubjectivity. The contrast between my relative safety in Greece and the realities my Ukrainian colleagues faced deepened my awareness to the unspoken dynamics within the group. This disparity heightened my sensitivity in interactions, compelling me to engage with profound respect and humility.

responseSet.title: Reflections on the Good Object
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reflections on the Good Object

Olena Uzunova-Tsebenoha MA

Olena Uzunova-Tsebenoha

Odesa, Ukraine

Olena Uzunova-Tsebenoha  

The topic of the good object, its role, reconstruction, and “creation” has always fascinated me. I once believed this subject had been thoroughly researched and described. However, my work during the war has revealed there is still much we do not know. In this essay, I aim to explore one aspect of the good object when a person experiences a loss of safety during wartime. Interestingly, in conditions where life is under threat for a prolonged period, individuals appear to regress to an earlier stage of development. To better illustrate this, I will share a brief clinical vignette.

Vignette

One of my clients fled Ukraine at the onset of Russia’s full-scale invasion, avoiding direct exposure to shelling. She found refuge and safety in a European country, where she still resides. In Ukraine, both she and I lived in Odesa, a city that, while not on the front line, has faced fierce attacks. Despite this, Odesa has not been destroyed and continues to function as a vibrant city. My client and I rarely discuss the war directly during our sessions.

During one session, my client expressed anger at me for returning to Odesa and remaining there for some time. When I asked what she feared, she became agitated and yelled that I had returned to a “dead city, completely destroyed and empty.” I tried to reassure her by saying the city had come back to life, many people had returned, and we were being protected by our military.

However, my words only intensified her distress, and I chose to remain silent.

Later, in the second year of her therapy, she began describing the European country where she now resides as safe, providing her with a sense of well-being.

She insisted she would never return to her “dead city,” where there were “no living beings and no work opportunities.” When I asked her to elaborate, she explained that her work in Ukraine had been tied to the port, which she believed was destroyed and would never function again. Surprised, I gently explained that the port was operational, supported by agreements such as the “grain deal” facilitated by our government. However, she responded with disbelief and insisted that “everything is dead.

responseSet.title: From Witness to With-Ness: Supervising in Ukraine
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Witness to With-Ness: Supervising in Ukraine

Rachel Sopher LCSW

Rachel Sopher

New York, New York, USA

Rachel Sopher  

Sitting on the sofa in my office on a random, cold, Monday afternoon, I flip open my screen. Seventeen little squares with seventeen little faces. Who are you? I wonder. Where are you? Are you okay? Are you safe?

We settle into our three hour zoom meeting – them presenting, me supervising, reflecting, all of us commenting, trying to mold unwieldy clinical narratives into some logical shape, trying through barriers of disparate language, culture, and physical distance to make sense of them.

These therapists, living and working in Ukraine, have decided to do something wild and quixotic – to enter a training program in psychoanalytic psychotherapy in the midst of the chaos of war, in the midst of the destruction of their culture, their way of life. In the midst of the inhumane, they have chosen to invest in humanity, in connection, in the future: in the wish for something more.

And yet.

There seems something fundamentally absurd about this endeavor, about in-depth psychoanalysis in a time of crisis management.

And another absurdity. This one mine alone:

Here I sit in my New York office. Comfy, well-fed, the illusion of safety intact, at least for this next while. The disjunction between their world and my own hits like a punch to the gut. Every clinical presentation includes the words “full invasion;” there are traumas here I know not of experiences far away. As our discussions go on, I am reminded, again and again, of the tenderness of human life, its fragilities, its precariousness.

The Ukrainian facilitator stops our session. The news agency just sent out a warning – there are bombs dropping. “If anyone is unsafe, or if anyone feels unsafe, you can leave this meeting,” she says. “Please leave this meeting.” No one does. I do not know how we carry on from here. But we do – Freud, Kohut, Bion, Mitchell – somehow our conversation goes on.

From Witness to With-Ness

As our meetings continue, I notice something strange – there is something impersonal, something tense in the field, and I don’t know why. The presenters ask me not to interrupt them, and I don’t know why. What I know how to offer feels like an intrusion. I don’t understand. I cannot make myself helpful.

[[RunningHead]]

Tu n'as rien vu en Haäti: Discussion of Shari Appollon's Paper

Andrew Asibong PHD

Andrew Asibong

19 Bloomsbury Square, London WC1A 2NS, UK
andrewasibongtherapy@gmail.com

Andrew Asibong   Psychodynamic psychotherapist and psycho-cultural theorist based in Brighton, England, whose recent publications include Post-Traumatic Attachments to the Eerily Moving Image: Something to Watch Over Me (Routledge, 2022) and “Circles of Sorrow: Discussion of David Eng’s and Shinhee Han’s ‘Racial Rage, Racial Guilt: the Uses of Anger in Asian America’”(Psychoanalytic Dialogues, 2024).

Using Shari Appollon’s article in this issue as a point of departure, I reflect on the phenomenon of shared reverie between analyst and patient, noting its quasi-fantastical dimension, something that feels simultaneously rooted in and transcendent of uncanny presence and absence. I go on to discuss post-Greenian approaches to psychic aliveness in the wake of relational deadening and dissociation, before exploring my own use of an eerie transitional object – a long-buried novel by the exiled Haitian writer Marie Vieux Chauvet – in a mind-altering process of post-traumatic self-resuscitation.

responseSet.title: “I Don’t Want This Knowledge”: The Aftermath of a Natural Disaster
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“I Don’t Want This Knowledge”: The Aftermath of a Natural Disaster

Ghislaine Boulanger PHD

Ghislaine Boulanger

30 Morningside Drive, Apartment 208, New York, NY 10025, USA
Ghislaine242@gmail.com

Ghislaine Boulanger   Psychologist and psychoanalyst in private practice in New York City. She is a graduate and a member of the Relational faculty of the New York University’s Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis and on the faculty of Adelphi University’s Specialization in Trauma Studies. The topics of particular interest to her concern the psychodynamic treatment of immigrants, psychoanalytic politics, and massive psychic trauma. Since the publication of Wounded by Reality: Understanding and Treating Adult Onset Trauma, Dr. Boulanger has taught and published extensively on the psychodynamic dilemmas facing adults who have survived violent and life-threatening events, and the clinicians who work with them.

The twentieth anniversary of Hurricane Katrina provides an opportunity to describe an Applied Psychoanalytic program that began with a grant to the Psychoanalytic Center in New Orleans three years after the hurricane, just as life appeared to be settling down for the survivors and yet the symptoms of Adult Onset Trauma were becoming more pronounced. In a previously unpublished talk that she gave to an audience of local mental health professional toward the beginning of this program, the author describes what she was learning from them about the ways in which their lives had changed since the hurricane, and their concerns about working under these circumstances. She described how the symptoms of Adult Onset Trauma might unfold over the next few years and how their program hoped to address these issues. In the final section of the current paper, the author reflects on changes in federal disaster preparedness that are being introduced by the Trump administration, along with a shocking celebration of the absence of empathy. She proposes that in these uncertain and ungenerous times, there is a particular place for applied psychoanalytic programs to offer therapeutic communities a very different kind of recovery work.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fucking Free

Adam Blum Psy.D.

Adam Blum

222 Columbus Ave, Suite 406, San Francisco, CA 94133, USA
adamblumpsyd@gmail.com

Adam Blum   Co-author of Here I’m Alive: The Spirit of Music in Psychoanalysis (Columbia University Press, 2023). He has written and presented on psychoanalysis and the music of Björk, Kendrick Lamar, Frank Ocean, Stephen Sondheim, Radiohead, and Michael Jackson. Recent publications include “Potential Time: The Music of Potential Space” (Psychoanalytic Quarterly, in press) and “The Eros Tour” (Psychoanalytic Inquiry, 2025). He is in private practice in San Francisco, where he teaches and supervises at the San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis and the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California.

This essay reads Sam Guzzardi’s “Laplanche in the Consulting Room” as a reminder that theory in psychoanalysis is not ornament but experience, and that writing itself can be a form of clinical presence. Following as Guzzardi courts the sexual drive without abandoning care, the discussion accompanies the reader through both theory and clinical material in which style speaks louder than technique, and wherein the freedom to be driven by the sexual is reconciled with the ghosts it inevitably awakens. What emerges is an irreducibly Laplanchean wager: that analysis works best not only when it unsettles or disturbs but also when it manages, somehow, to keep the faith we need to mourn our lives, sustaining and releasing us into freer futures.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thinking Race: Traversing Perilous Terrain with Bernadine Han

Alexandra Woods PHD

Alexandra Woods

New York, New York, USA
Awoodsphd@gmail.com

Alexandra Woods   Maintains a small private practice with adults. She has supervised at City College Doctoral Program in Clinical Psychology for twenty years. She is a member of Black Psychoanalysts Speak. She has published articles on the interplay of whiteness and the psychoanalytic institute (Psychoanalysis, Culture & Society (2020) 25, 230–249), on white privilege and its fissures (in Stoute, B. and Slevin, M. (eds.) 2022 The Trauma or Racism; Lessons from the Therapeutic Encounter) and on theories of activism. Since 2017, she has expanded her political activism. She derives solace and pleasure in nature and combines memoir, clinical work and experiences of the natural world in her writing (Winter into spring in The Room 6.25.)

This discussion engages personally with Bernadine Han’s experiential and visceral portrayal of being the recipient of a cascading series of racialized microaggressions. The discussion highlights key aspects of the author’s complex theoretical analysis of such experiences and their relation to a dominant, unacknowledged, ideology of whiteness and white supremacy. The discussion includes examples of similar events, and it further explores the connection between “micro” and “macro” aggressions. The discussant uses a socio-historical perspective to consider how individuals and groups defined as “other” are punished for daring to become subjects in their own right.

responseSet.title: Looking Up at the Stars from the Middle of the Lake: Dare We Speak of Spirit? A Model of Psychedelic Consciousness for Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Looking Up at the Stars from the Middle of the Lake: Dare We Speak of Spirit? A Model of Psychedelic Consciousness for Psychoanalysis

David Raniere PHD

David Raniere

Brookline, Massachusetts, USA
161 Worcester Road, Suite 201, Framingham, MA 01701, USA
davidranierephd@gmail.com

David Raniere   Clinical psychologist and psychoanalyst in private practice in Framingham, MA, and a licensed natural medicine (psilocybin) facilitator in Colorado. He is a graduate of the Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis (MIP) where he serves as Faculty and Supervising Analyst.

This discussion extends psychoanalytic theory and practice to engage expanded states of consciousness elicited by psychedelic medicine. The author utilizes Peoples and Rundel’s clinical illustration involving ketamine-assisted psychoanalysis to propose the intra-collective as a transpersonal holding environment that encompasses and contains the intersubjective field, where highly organized, undifferentiated, and developmentally mature psychic states transpire. The author argues that a novel lexicon is needed to access and commune with unitive experiences beyond the traditional analytic register. He speaks of Spirit to establish a pathway to this non-dualistic level of consciousness, and to preserve sacred experiences that exist beyond the symbolic realm.

responseSet.title: Un(Thinking) Race, Resisting Knowing, Restoring the Social Third
responseSet.newSectionName: Discussion of Bernadine H. Han's article
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Un(Thinking) Race, Resisting Knowing, Restoring the Social Third

Daniel José Gaztambide Psy.D.

Daniel José Gaztambide

Rm A344, 65-30 Kissena Blvd., Queens, NY 11367
daniel.gaztambide@qc.cuny.edu

Daniel José Gaztambide   Assistant professor of psychology at Queens College, where he is the director of the Frantz Fanon Lab for Decolonial Psychology, and a faculty member in the Department of Critical Psychology at the City University of New York (CUNY) Graduate Center. He is the author of the books A People’s History of Psychoanalysis: From Freud to Liberation Psychology, and the recent Decolonizing Psychoanalytic Technique: Putting Freud on Fanon’s Couch, which received a 2024 Gradiva Award for Best Book. He is in analytic training at the New York University (NYU) Post-Doctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, and he is the recipient of multiple fellowships including a Mellon Foundation Fellowship and a Miranda Family Fellowship for his research on colonial mentality and the application of psychoanalytic treatment to diverse populations. His recent article, “Standing Against Racial Capitalism: Reconsidering Psychology’s Role in Dismantling Systemic Racism,” was published in a recent special issue in American Psychologist on addressing racism in psychology. This work is an extension of his service as a Taskforce member at the APA’s Taskforce on Strategies for the Elimination of Racism, Discrimination, and Hate, for which he received a presidential citation for his work.

Using personal reflections and theoretical analysis, this article engages Bernadine Han’s excellent essay “Thinking Race: Exploring the Scene of Racial Enactment.” The author shares his resonance with Han’s experience in psychoanalytic spaces, weaving in and out of the impact of her writing on his subjectivity and the reminiscences it evokes from him as a Puerto Rican analytic candidate. Resonating with Han’s subjective experience while also following her thinking, as a fellow traveler, the author makes some analytic observations about the limitations of using traditional psychoanalytic theories to understand and critique racism and inequality. Specifically, the extent to which psychoanalysis—relational psychoanalysis in particular—betrayed a “dyadic default,” the tendency to reduce psychosocial phenomena to a primarily two-person experience, and to recenter a two-person “dyadic logic.” The interplay between this logic and its default is argued to limit our imagination for addressing structural and collective problems in psychoanalysis as a treatment, a mode of training, and a community. The author concludes with the implications of Han’s thinking for how we address such tensions not as individuals, not as pairs, but as a group, the necessity of thinking as a “we,” and the importance of creating such a sense of “we” for analytic candidates of color.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Inviting and Meeting the Hyper-Real: Response to Woods and Gaztambide

Bernadine H. Han MD

Bernadine H. Han

45 Plaza Street W, Ste 1A, Brooklyn, NY 11217, USA
beh9052@med.cornell.edu
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-0157-2214

Bernadine H. Han   Assistant clinical professor of psychiatry at Weill Cornell Medical College, and faculty and curriculum co-chair at the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research. She has a private practice in Brooklyn, NY, and is also on staff at South Beach Psychiatric Center’s outpatient clinics.

This paper responds to those by Woods and Gaztambide, reflecting on and wrestling with the challenge of institutional containment and the problem of repair. Inspired by the risk accepted by them to think race together, I also consider the analytic dyad’s necessary commitment to the perilous and vitalizing felt real to find meeting and possibility.

[[RunningHead]]

Notes from the Après Coup, or, Jammin’ Out and Fuckin’ Free

Sam Guzzardi LCSW

Sam Guzzardi

32 Union Square East, Suite, New York, NY 1106, USA
sgpsychotherapy@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-9191-1007

Sam Guzzardi   New York City–based psychoanalyst trained at the Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity, IPSS. His written work on gender and sexuality in psychoanalysis has won numerous awards, including the Ralph Roughton Award of the American Psychoanalytic Association and, most recently, the 2025 Tiresias Award of the International Psychoanalytical Association. He is on the faculty of the National Institute of the Psychotherapies (NIP) and IPSS and is an associate editor at Psychoanalysis, Self, and Context and at Psychoanalytic Inquiry. His clinical work focuses on questions of gender, trauma, development, sexuality, and loss.

This paper explores the author’s affective experience following responses to essays by Jade McGleughlin, Adam Blum, and Daniel Butler. The paper returns to Laplanche’s concept of the sole hermeneut and the author’s evolving understanding of allowing the patient—or in this case, the respondents—to interpret and build upon their ideas. Through reflections on personal experiences, clinical work, and the insights of the respondents, the author examines the complex interplay of freedom, surrender, and connection in the psychoanalytic process. The author expresses gratitude to those who contributed to the project, acknowledging the “lytic” experience of releasing the work and the potential for transformation inherent in the act of interpretation.

responseSet.title: Expansion and Regression in Oceanic Experience: A Model of Psychedelic Consciousness for Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Expansion and Regression in Oceanic Experience: A Model of Psychedelic Consciousness for Psychoanalysis

Karen Peoples PHD and Megan Rundel PHD

Megan Rundel

San Francisco, California, USA

San Francisco, California, USA
3824 Grand Avenue Oakland, CA 94610, USA
meganrundel@gmail.com

Megan Rundel   Clinical Psychologist and a Personal and Supervising Analyst and Faculty at the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California. She is also a graduate of the Psychedelic Assisted Therapies and Research program at the California Institute of Integral Studies, as well as a number of ketamine training programs. She has taught and written widely on the use of psychedelics in enhancing psychoanalysis; her most recent paper is “Psychedelic Psychoanalysis: Transformations of the Self,” published in Psychoanalytic Dialogues in 2022. She maintains a private practice of psychoanalysis and consultation in Oakland, California.

In this paper, we take up the question of what happens from a psychoanalytic perspective when a patient engages in psychedelic-assisted therapy. While some writers have suggested that psychedelic agents promote regression to an earlier point in development, we argue that such states may be better understood as expanding the mind beyond ordinary consciousness. We assert that traditional models of psychoanalytic regression applied to psychedelic states do not adequately align with findings from psychedelic neuroscience or from infant observation research. Instead, we see states of consciousness from the beginning of life and throughout the lifecycle as fluctuating on a continuum between discursive, highly differentiated cognitive processes, on one end, through dreamlike, liminal states of awareness, to unitive experiences of oneness, on the other. These are considered part of a normative spectrum of consciousness. We explore oceanic experiences as a unique expanded state of consciousness from a new psychoanalytic perspective and highlight the way these experiences under psychedelics can be a powerful force for healing. A case example of a patient who used ketamine as part of his analytic treatment is offered to illustrate our theoretical points.

responseSet.title: Expansion without Erasure: A Psychoanalytic Response to Peoples and Rundel
responseSet.newSectionName: Discussion of Karen Peoples and Megan Rundel's article
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Expansion without Erasure: A Psychoanalytic Response to Peoples and Rundel

Daniel A. Brenner MD

Daniel A. Brenner

6 Bigelow Street, Cambridge, MA 02139, USA
dan@cbio.health

Daniel A. Brenner   Psychiatrist and psychoanalyst, and the Founder and Medical Director of Cambridge Biotherapies, a multi-site treatment center specializing in ketamine-assisted psychotherapy, ketamine infusion, and transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS). He earned a B.A. from Harvard University and an M.D. from Tufts University School of Medicine. He completed his psychiatry residency at Cambridge Health Alliance, where he served as Chief Resident in the Outpatient Department, and was later a Fellow in the Program for Psychotherapy. A graduate of the Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute, Dr. Brenner served as a Clinical Instructor in Psychiatry (2000–2010) at Harvard Medical School, and continues there as a Teaching Associate. He maintains a private psychotherapy practice in Cambridge, Massachusetts, working with adult and adolescent patients.

Peoples and Rundel argue that oceanic experiences in psychedelic-assisted treatment need not indicate regression but may reflect expansions of consciousness. I respond by examining how the self enables survival yet contributes to suffering, distinguishing expanded states from perspective, and proposing perspective as the hinge that transforms transient ego-dissolution into lasting growth. I differentiate pain from suffering and note how ketamine’s modulation of the default mode and anti-reward networks uniquely fosters equanimity, opening analytic space. Because psychedelics can overwhelm defenses, preparation, and set and setting remain decisive. I propose integration practices that consolidate the observing stance—variously described as observing ego, thirdness, mindful awareness, or Atman. Psychedelic psychoanalysis, I suggest, builds upon rather than abandons analytic commitments, translating oceanic states into enduring capacities for reflection, equanimity, and relatedness. I close with a clinical vignette and proposals for distinguishing restorative union from defensive fusion, sequencing ketamine-assisted analysis, and sustaining perspective.

responseSet.title: In This Issue, Psychoanalytic Dialogues 35–3
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In This Issue, Psychoanalytic Dialogues 35–3

Stephen Hartman PHD and Lauren Levine PHD

Lauren Levine

Lauren Levine  

In this issue, Psychoanalytic Dialogues, a journal of diverse, international and interdisciplinary relational perspectives, affirms its commitment to Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion. Our mission is clear and unchanged.

Dialogues has championed open and uncensored conversation among thought leaders in psychoanalysis and allied disciplines since its very first issue 35 years ago, and we will not preemptively comply with the demands of any political regime that exerts closure on intellectual thought and scientific research.

Dialogues will not police its editorial policy to titrate our authors’ freedom of speech despite edicts that threaten funding for anyone who reckons with personal and social traumas that span generations or who voices concern for, in Franz Fanon’s words, the wretched of the earth. Nor will we shelter our readers from uncomfortable debates about theoretical, clinical, and political controversies knowing, as our founders did, that our field grows in the encounter with difference.

We will continue to publish first-rate contributions to scholarship on the gamut of psychoanalytic themes and relational controversies that collectively proffer a vision of rigor and expansive thought in this time of collapsed core human values and an assault on truth and the Earth itself.

At a time when many communities face violence and marginalization, our editorial policy will not concede to a zeitgeist that concedes to the foreclosure of gender expression in its fullness nor to the complexity of racial expression and the celebration of difference among people of diverse ethnicities, cultures, national and regional identities, linguistic backgrounds, and abilities. We will not be silent about injustice whether it came to the fore clinically in the telescoping of generations or fulminated in social context. Nor will we stifle the exploration of gender and sexual expression and reproductive freedom. We will not comply with any demand that reparation for racial inequity and genocidal trauma be whitewashed, nor will we espouse mythological freedoms that, as we have seen in recent days, amount to no less than the decimation of inquiry, the ruination of the global economy, and the collapse of thinking itself.

responseSet.title: Introduction
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction

Stephen Hartman PHD and Lauren Levine PHD

Lauren Levine

Lauren Levine  

In perhaps his most famous essay, Attacks on Linking (1959), Wilfred Bion charted how destructive forces in the psyche mitigate against processes of thinking and emotional integration. When painful or confusing emotions threaten to overwhelm the ego, Bion proposed that attacks on linking defensively destroy meaning and curtail insight. This destructiveness has a total quality; it is an attack on thinking and on thought itself. Then, the analyst’s task is to contain projected bits of unmetabolized perception which becomes a struggle to restore the capacity for thinking at the most basic level. We asked our contributors to revisit Bion’s influential essay as they experience it in this moment when attacks on core human values and social norms are tragically ubiquitous as is an attack on the Earth itself.

Stephen Hartman, Ph.D. and Lauren Levine, Ph.D.

Joint Editors-in-Chief, Psychoanalytic Dialogues

293

responseSet.title: On Attacks on Linking
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Attacks on Linking

Leopoldo Caravedo MA

Leopoldo Caravedo

Lima, Peru

Leopoldo Caravedo  

Human life is an exchange; it is a relationship. From the simplest to the most complex elements, these exchanges aim to develop something more elaborate that transcends us and gives us a greater capacity to respond to the challenges that human beings must face. This is how we advanced as a species, adapting, developing capacities, building community, protecting ourselves, caring for each other, constituting and reciprocally configuring ourselves in the links we form.

We are witnessing how devastating it can be when the possibility of exchange, social pact, mutual collaboration, dialogue, and respect for the rules of coexistence is broken in a language more akin to our daily lives: when the link is attacked. And this attack can take place in different ways, some more openly violent, some rather more veiled.

José Saramago said that trying to convince another is like colonizing their mind. Placing ideas, beliefs, objects, etc., that seem to be one’s own in the other’s mind is a way of neutralizing the other’s initiatives, their otherness, and appropriating their desire. This is a form of attack on linking that is often invisible but very powerful.

In a slightly less recognizable way, all the maneuvers seek to prevent the production and expression of emotions and affects that connect with others. In human relationships, emotions and affections allow us to articulate our thoughts, memories, traditions, beliefs, desires, etc. Together with language, which conveys their expression, they support the mediation between minds. Human development is impossible if emotion and thought go separate ways. It is already complex when they do not spontaneously relate to each other, and it is excruciating when some form of power expressly sets out to prevent them from connecting.

Attacking the link is like cutting, interrupting the cables that connect the lighting poles, which would leave the community without light, without means of communication, and without the ability to transmit and receive information from their environment and to others. It would be living in darkness.

responseSet.title: Sobre el Ataque al Vinculo
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sobre el Ataque al Vinculo

Leopoldo Caravedo MA

Leopoldo Caravedo

Lima, Peru

Leopoldo Caravedo  

La vida humana es un intercambio, es relación. Desde sus elementos más simples a los más complejos, esos intercambios apuntan a desarrollar algo más elaborado que nos trascienda, con más capacidad de responder a los retos que el ser humano debe afrontar. Así fuimos avanzando como especie, adaptándonos, desarrollando capacidades, construyendo comunidad, protegiéndonos, cuidándonos, constituyéndonos y configurándonos recíprocamente en los vínculos que vamos formando

Estamos siendo testigos en estos tiempos, de lo devastador que puede ser cuando esa posibilidad de intercambio, de pacto social, de mutua colaboración, de diálogo y respeto de las reglas de convivencia, se quiebra; en un lenguaje más afín a nuestro quehacer: cuando se ataca el vínculo. Y este ataque se puede dar de distintas maneras, algunas más abiertamente violentas, algunas más veladas.

José Saramago decía que tratar de convencer a otro es como colonizar su mente. Colocar en la mente del otro ideas, creencias, objetos, etc, que parezcan propios, es una forma de neutralizar las iniciativas del otro, su alteridad, de apropiarse de su deseo. Una forma de ataque al vínculo muchas veces invisible pero muy potente.

De forma un poco menos reconocible, están todas las maniobras que buscan impedir que se produzcan y expresen emociones y afectos que conecten con los otros. En las relaciones humanas, las emociones y los afectos permiten articular nuestros pensamientos, recuerdos, tradiciones, creencias, deseos, etc. Junto con la palabra, que vehiculiza su expresión, son los soportes de la mediación entre las mentes. El desarrollo humano es imposible si la emoción y el pensamiento van por separado. Ya es complejo cuando espontáneamente no se relacionan, y es lacerante cuando alguna forma de poder se propone expresamente el impedir que se conecten.

Atacar el vínculo es como cortar, interrumpir, los cables que conectan los postes que llevan el alumbrado, lo que dejaría a cualquier comunidad sin luz, sin medios de comunicación, sin poder transmitir y recibir la información de su entorno y hacia los otros. Se viviría en la penumbra.

responseSet.title: Bion’s “Attacks on Linking” and the Roots of Ethical Life
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bion’s “Attacks on Linking” and the Roots of Ethical Life

Giuseppe Civitarese MD PHD

Giuseppe Civitarese

Pavia, Italy
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-3466-4329

Giuseppe Civitarese  

Bion took seriously the suggestion of his analyst – the same one who, a year earlier, had hoped someone would write about linking – and produced a true gem of psychoanalytic literature: a remarkably brief essay, yet among the most cited of all time. But how could psychoanalysis, a discipline so preoccupied with relationships, have felt the need in 1959 to explicitly ponder the notion of “linking,” a term whose immediate meaning already suggests relation?

One might argue that the analytic bond was regarded – and perhaps still is, despite theoretical advances – primarily as a study of the other, without fully acknowledging its deeply intersubjective dimension. Yet in Klein’s groundbreaking ideas – where dreaming parallels playing and symbolizing – and in the invention of projective identification, one can already sense the emergence of intersubjectivity. However, in Klein’s approach, the analyst does not play with the child: the child plays, while the analyst interprets the unconscious fantasies expressed through that play. Bion’s further step in Attacks on Linking (AL) is precisely to frame linking as an authentically intersubjective process.

AL itself is like a fine red wine, revealing its complexity gradually. If the bottle remains Klein-inspired, it is as though projective identification were viewed under an electron microscope, emphasizing its communicative – and therefore bidirectional – function. Enter bizarre objects, obstructive objects, and mysterious “visible-invisible” hallucinations, both fascinating and unsettling.

I believe the essence of this essay is akin to Poe’s “Purloined Letter:” it slips past us precisely because it is right before our eyes. To clarify: taking seriously the concept of “bond” – expressed in English significantly through the verb “linking,” rather than the noun “link” – has an ontological resonance. Linking is the fabric of being: the dance of electrons, lovers in an embrace, the prayers of the faithful, the shout at a goal scored, the word that stirs the soul, the gaze that comforts, the rope joining climbers, the knowing nod of understanding. It is the endless conversation of Being, experienced phenomenologically as existence, intuition, vision, and openness to the world.
