responseSet.title: Group Methods for Attacking Thoughts and Mangling Perception
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Group Methods for Attacking Thoughts and Mangling Perception

Karim G. Dajani Psy.D.

Karim G. Dajani

Washington, D.C., USA

Karim G. Dajani  

We are in late March of 2025. Bombs keep falling on people who have been starved, maimed and displaced before being incinerated. The missiles are lobbed by drones and airplanes from the sky. The people burn, the ones who “make it” gather their children’s body parts. We are watching it live. Those of us who speak up are met with violence. Mahmood Khalil, a legal U.S resident, is languishing in a jail for speaking up. This is the land of the free where we can speak our minds and live our lives in difference. That is except if you are a Palestinian or if you speak the obvious – Israel is conducting a genocide in Gaza and the U.S is aiding and abetting it. When you speak some version of these words, you are met with violence on a bad day and a deafening silence on every other day.

One of my patients is a wealthy Palestinian-American man with young children. He cannot understand how his children are worth so much less that his wealthy Christian and Jewish neighbors. He scrolls down his Instagram page to witness the death of thousands of children in Gaza who were starved and maimed before being incinerated. He tells his neighbors what he is seeing. He is met with silence. A silence that tells him to be quiet, to stop seeing and feeling because it might disturb our enjoyment of the many luxuries that define our life.

The assault on free speech as it relates to Palestine is not confined to Palestine, it is systemic. It is now an assault on trans individuals and trans care, it is an assault on women’s bodies (abortion restrictions), it is an assault on immigrants (millions of people are slated for deportation and the people at the borders are being imprisoned, caged, and harmed), it is an assault on the institutions of democracy (the education department, the Kennedy center, the FBI, CIA, and the justice department are all being gutted and reshaped along ideological lines), it is an assault on the media (Maggie Haberman a Pulitzer prize NYT reporter was just referred to by the President of the U.S as “Maggot Hagerman the really dumb scammer”). These are but a few examples.

responseSet.title: Being the Brick: Notes Toward Thinking About the Clinical Utility of Trans Negativity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Being the Brick: Notes Toward Thinking About the Clinical Utility of Trans Negativity

Griffin Hansbury L.C.S.W.-R.

Griffin Hansbury

New York, New York, USA

Griffin Hansbury  

In today’s trans-antagonistic backlash to increased rights and visibility, transness is scapegoated, framed as thought virus, a social contagion spread through the hyperlinked internet. When the backlash began, around 2015, the right-wing attacks focused on physical space, like bathrooms, and while they still target such spaces, they have engineered a new tactic: Attack the links to thinking trans. At breakneck speed, the administration has scrubbed federal websites, deleting links that mention transgender, gender, or simply the letter “T.” (On the National Park Service’s site for the Stonewall Monument, you can read about “LGB civil rights,” a familiar acronym dismembered – to obstruct re-membering.) One executive order enforces “patriotic education” to stop trans “indoctrination” by defunding schools that teach “gender ideology.” What we cannot think, they suppose, we will not be.

Trans people have risen up in resistance. The Transexual Menace, a 1990s activist organization, has returned, reprinting their Rocky Horror-style t-shirts, revivifying the twentieth-century’s “bad trans object” (Keegan, 2019), which is good. At Stonewall rallies, calling back queer riot, activists dislodge bricks from the sidewalk and hold them aloft, with the slogan, “Be the brick.” While no bricks have yet been thrown, the idea is in the air – with its accompanying affects, a collective gesture forming a “trans affective commons” (Malatino, 2022).

As the state attacks links that connect us to society and its processes of normalization, trans people may re-link to trans negativity – a refusal of hegemonic happiness, an embrace of otherness and its bad feelings as empowerment, connection, and resistance.

In the earlier 2000s, neoliberalism deployed trans-normalization to de-link trans from negativity. TIME announced “the transgender tipping point” into the mainstream and trans people, mostly transfeminine, appeared in more television shows, movies, and advertising. My transition began in the late 1980s and early 1990s, when we were encouraged to live as “stealth,” and I now found myself expanding, taking up space as openly trans. I didn’t just feel safer, I felt more thinkable. As a cis-assumed trans man, in a time when transmasculinity had barely been imagined, I had always been illegible, unthinkable to most. Now I could be thought, seen, even valued. But something about it chafed. Trans-normalization and positivity, working for patriarchy, white supremacy, and capitalism, seduces us away from queerness, our outsider againstness, and negative affects like trans melancholia and rage. (Melancholia, we’re told, is mourning gone wrong, while anger is a secondary emotion to be overcome, transformed into something productive.) I was a trans “success,” but still not happy, and the unhappy queer is a problem (Ahmed, 2010). I was too attached to my “trans maladjustment” (Awkward-Rich, 2022) and the world kept telling me to get over it. The past was past, they said, and look! Heartland America was crowning trans girls as prom queen. It did get better! But the past is never past. And the Heartland wasn’t as embracing as some wanted to believe.

responseSet.title: The Ineffable Effable
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Ineffable Effable

Shifa Haq PHD

Shifa Haq

New Delhi, India
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-0507-7563

Shifa Haq  

Being bitten by a snake is not a memory one forgets easily. My father did not. He recounts that he was nine or ten years old when, in the middle of the night, he got bitten by a snake. As pain and dizziness began to take hold of him, the village elders responded with panic and wisdom in equal measures. Snake sighting or bites were after all not so uncommon. He does not remember very much except that those attending to him kept pouring cold water from the well nearby to keep him “awake.” When I think about this memory, I imagine him as a child in the grips of a terrifying and incommunicable state, that of the fear of dying. One can imagine that in the memory is the memory of being neither dead nor alive. The cold splashes of water that prevented him from fading into a venomous sleep create a caesura, or a raging river between two banks of the psychic experience of life and death (Bergstein, 2013). Like snakebites, the bite on the analyst’s linking function that make her a human soup, in Bion’s words, is not so uncommon. I think of the times when the analysis appears to be dead in the water. The image came back to me while working with Samar, as I began confronting chaotic and fragmented material that she offered as an elegy for the unbearable states of mind. Like my father’s memory, I appeared to be going in and out the session only to be brought back by flashes of her voice imploring me to not give up. I would repeatedly find myself confused and helpless. In a way, it was easy to lose sight of the terror that she was experiencing. Such emotional turbulence, thought Bion (1982), requires that we move “without gripping onto any of the banks in a way that halts movement” (p. 625). Samar’s inchoate states started to form a language of pain in migraines. Over several sessions a storm of associations or day material came pouring from her – all pointing toward a cold, intrusive and exhausting world to live in. During this time, she began caring for feral kittens who, like her, were struggling to survive an inhabitable world: Samar’s neighborhood had silenced stories of cats that had been poisoned by an unknown resident; there was an air of hostility toward strays making it difficult for those like her who care to feed them; the kittens she was looking after, climbed up trees and balconies from where they were irretrievable; later, they developed a deadly infection requiring quarantining for days. A question emerged in her mind – will they make it out alive? Assailed by incoherence, I notice myself becoming passive. It became difficult to think about what is going on in “this neighbourhood.” The attacks on links emerged in the session as the story, of “the ineffable effable” in T. S. Elliot’s words. Like Macavity, the mystery cat, the Napolean of crime, my patient beguiled me to reach the scene of crime only to find the patient “not there” (Eliot, 1939). Perhaps the patient was reliving the failure of communication, the constrictions of my mind (as in the migraine) that did not allow movement of the slippery, foreign or feral objects that flooded her in hope of a rescue by the maternal reverie. The patient knew, at some level, that her wish to make a creative contact was met by an obstructive object, that was sometimes in her, sometimes in me, or an unknown location (Bergstein, 2015; Bion, 1957). Through a relational intuition, I learned to understand that this was her drive, through attacks on linking, to communicate an emotional experience of what it is to be with an other. She advocated against animal neglect and animal cruelty, telling me more and more about what humans do to animals. Slowly I began to stay awake to children’s zoopoetics, tracing what analysts do to patients or mothers do to infants. Samar was not performing wokeness. She was learning from experience.

responseSet.title: Stuttering
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Stuttering

Gohar Homayounpour Psy.D.

Gohar Homayounpour

Paris, France

Gohar Homayounpour  

Aren’t we all somehow the toad buried alive under the stone?

This is an image offered by Frau Emmy von N, one of Freud’s first patients, in his 1895 case study with Josef Breuer as a metaphor for her psychic position. Emmy’s image of the toad is one that has never left me. For me, it captures not only what becomes of the psyche through trauma, excruciating losses, and unimaginable circumstances, but what our psychic apparatus à la fin at times precisely wishes for. To be still, silent, unexposed. To be spared the friction of connection, of linking, of binding and the ambivalence of desire. To be released of linking, of pleasure, of love, and of the erotic.

The buried/alive toad is not simply a victim; the unfortunate situation she is finding herself in is part and parcel of the core and essence of her being. It is an unconscious fantasy, a way of surviving just beneath life, in retreat from the unbearable risk of fully being alive. For within Freudian metapsychology there is nothing more horrifying than pleasure.

The wish is not to die, but to attack linking, to slip beneath the weight of the symbolic, to no longer be a fully speaking/desiring subject.

Bion’s (1959) “Attacks on Linking”: “the destructive attacks which the patient makes on anything which is felt to have the function of linking one object with another” (p. 308). So not on the object but on linking. To link, for Bion, is to bring thoughts and feelings into relation, to allow meaning/thinking to form and transform. But there are times when that capacity is handicapped not merely under unbearable or traumatic events but under the most ordinary of life’s circumstances, when our psychic apparatus, unconsciously wishes to unbind, to sever its own capacity to think, to symbolize, to be in relation to an “Other.” This is what I wish for us all to acknowledge first and foremost, this human, all too human, condition inherent in our being. There is a fundamental inescapable wish in all of us to disconnect, certainly the quantity and constellation varies, and the wish to “un-link” is magnified under traumatic situations.

responseSet.title: Unsent Letter to a Father
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Unsent Letter to a Father

Julie Leavitt MD

Julie Leavitt

San Francisco, California, USA

Julie Leavitt  

Attacks on linking afflict the mainstream collective unconscious, as when, to preserve power, supremist monocultures systematically mobilize hatred of a targeted Other across a community (or nation), with the violence of erasure. These attacks corrupt shared emotional relating between individuals: in the case of attacks on gender, between filial generations.

A Gender-Queer adult uses the epistolary form to elucidate linking attacks in their estranged relationship with their father, contextualized in the unfolding escalation of transphobic bigotry in the US. Here, the attacks on linking are threefold: interpersonal links between Queer children and their miscomprehending parents; internal links anchoring a person in their elaborately felt gender; and social links between an already gender-fluid society and a domineering (patriarchal) system bent on “one man and one woman” to maintain male supremacy, structural misogyny and transphobia.

The Letter

Interpersonal Attacks on Linking between Child and Father

Dear Father,

This is not an easy letter to write.

I’ve had many inner dialogues with you recently but, immobilized in futility, I’ve kept them inside. Throughout my life I’ve felt unknown and misunderstood by you in my Queerness. Any reckoning seems impossible now, in a political climate where you and I fall on opposite sides of a perilous divide.

Our last contact was in 2017: in an e-mail exchange I noted how our proud-colonial family immigration story, 11 generations on, would—ironically—be thwarted had we immigrated now with Trump’s border wall. Without inviting further discussion, your blunt reply was:

Don’t be so angry!

I responded:

You have no idea how angry I am, Dad. Angry, horrified and extremely sad. Disgusted and disappointed, disturbed, offended. Terrified and anxious. This is a terrible time for our country. The US is openly expressing our nationalist bigotry to the rest of the world. It’s a time to get angry. I believe it will lead to facing some hard facts about our less favorable history as a nation, and hopefully will lead to change, if we don’t destroy ourselves before we get there - I am losing confidence in that in the past 3 weeks.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Attacks on Linking: Subway Collisions and Connections

Dionne R. Powell MD

Dionne R. Powell

New York, New York, USA

Dionne R. Powell  

“How troubling it feels to feel anger!” My patient, T,1 begins her session with the following narrative: It’s a typical crowded rush hour weekday evening in New York City as T enters the subway, her forward advance is partially blocked by a man with a large backpack, when suddenly she is pushed from behind.

T:

“Don’t push me!”

Fellow Commutor1 (FC1):

“I don’t want to get stuck between the doors!”

T:

“And I don’t want to be pushed!”

FC1:

“You don’t have to say this to me!”

T:

“Push me again and we’ll have a problem!

responseSet.title: Against Literalism: The Sick City, Infanticide, and Attacks on Linking
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Against Literalism: The Sick City, Infanticide, and Attacks on Linking

Noha Sadek MD

Noha Sadek

New York, New York, USA

Noha Sadek  

In a recent conversation with a friend in Beirut in the aftermath of the brutal war that terrorized the country and turned his neighborhood and many others into rubble, I was struck by his frequent use of Arabic words denoting “insanity,” “madness,” “craziness,” and “lunacy,” in describing the people he was encountering in his daily life, on the streets, grocery stores, and other places. I felt that my friend – a deeply perceptive, mature, young man – was perhaps also trying his best to keep his mind together. I said how can these people not look “insane” when the world around them was the one going insane? I added, as if somehow to reassure him, that madness was a desperate response to the unbearable pain of living in a cruel world.

It is no wonder that Bion, who witnessed firsthand the horrors and madness of the first world war, offers us his concept of attacks on linking as a theory of the psychotic mode of thinking. Bion (1959/2013) writes primarily about psychotic modes of thinking in the context of mother–child and analyst–patient relationships. For Bion, when the parent’s ability to contain the infant’s bodily and mental states fails, when the parental “container” is impaired, the infant attacks the linking function in himself (his own mind) and in the parent because linking of sensations, thoughts and feelings leads to the truth about internal and external reality, something unbearable for an infant without a containing environment. Bion (1959/2013) also speaks of the infant/patient’s “inborn disposition to excessive destructiveness, hatred, and envy” (p. 297), what might be considered an intense death drive, which triggers unprovoked attacks on linking, a topic of psychoanalytic debate beyond the scope of this brief essay. These attacks, provoked or unprovoked, leave the individual in a fractured, fragmented, “psychotic” state of mind, which impairs curiosity and learning. Can we apply this concept beyond its originally intended intrapsychic and intersubjective contexts? I propose the Oedipal myth as a potential “link.

responseSet.title: De-Pathologizing Colonial Traumas and Colonial Lives: A Response to Shari Appollon
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

De-Pathologizing Colonial Traumas and Colonial Lives: A Response to Shari Appollon

Kris Yi Psy.D. PHD

Kris Yi

Los Angeles, 131 N. El Molino Ave Suite 220, Pasadena, CA 91011, USA
krisyiphd@gmail.com

Kris Yi   Clinical psychologist and psychoanalyst in private practice in Pasadena, California. She is an associate editor of the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association. She is a faculty member at the Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles.

Shari Appollon uses Eng and Han’s concept of depathologized racial melancholia (2000) to explore the impact of colonization by the West, anti-Black racism, migration, and personal loss on three generations of Haiti-American women (her grandmother, mother, and herself), and her work with Lola, an older Haitian-American woman. She argues that for Haitians, mourning lost objects and motherland is impossible, when the past and current colonial violence against Haiti and its diaspora in America, continues. She uses a shared reverie with her patient Lola, in which Haiti is imagined as pristine, as a form of resistance to colonial occupation. In my response to Shari’s work, I add observations of my own from a trauma-informed framework, to highlight psychic responses to colonial traumas that go beyond depression and melancholia. These include highly dysregulated affects, violent rage, concordant and complementary identification with the colonial aggressor. Furthermore, I respond to Shari’s exploration of the intergenerational transmission of the colonial traumas among the three generations of her Haitian American family, with an exploration of the impact of the colonial occupation – by a different colonial power, Japan – on the three generations of my own Korean American family.

A Haitian, a Korean, and a Mixed-Race Black Brit Walk into a Bar

Shari Appollon L.C.S.W.-R.

Shari Appollon

147 Prince Street #201, Brooklyn, NY 11201, USA
shari@talktoshari.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3839-532X

Shari Appollon   Haitian-American psychoanalyst in private practice in Brooklyn New York. She is a 2024 graduate of the National Institute for the Psychotherapies and a 2019 graduate of the OYP at The Stephen Mitchell Relational Study Center. Shari is the recipient of NIP’s 2020 Educator’s Award for her paper ‘The Triple Entendre’ and runner-up for Division 39’s 2020 Candidate Essay Contest for her paper ‘The Parable of the Sower’. She writes primarily about culture, psychoanalytic theory and enjoys engaging in herbalism.

In my reply to Asibong and Yi, I generate connecting points to each respondent while simultaneously attempting to bridge gaps where there is distance. Within the frame of how each interpreted my paper, I expand and constrict accordingly. I attempt to integrate aspects from each respondent, as it pertains to their impact on what I had hoped to communicate about depathologized melancholia in an effort to de-center the role of whiteness as an abstract psychic force-field binding the Other, and instead center the culture of the Other as that which can never truly be mourned, in the multifaceted ways that one connects to their home of origin.

responseSet.title: Telescopic Memories, Psychic Zones, Hidden Times*
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Telescopic Memories, Psychic Zones, Hidden Times*

Rina Lazar PHD

Rina Lazar

Ramot Naftali 9, Tel Aviv 6927862, Israel
rinalazar61@gmail.com

Rina Lazar   Clinical psychologist in private practice in Tel-Aviv. She is a senior teacher and supervisor in the Core Program, the Relational Track and the Ph.D. Studies in Psychoanalysis and its Interfaces, the Program of Psychotherapy, Sackler Faculty of Medicine, Tel Aviv University. She is a member of the International Association of Relational Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy (IARPP) and the first chairperson of the Israeli chapter of the IARPP. Rina is the editor of Talking about Evil (Routledge, 2017), and the co-editor of Desire and The Blind Spot (Hakibutz Hameuchad, 2005, 2007).

This paper focus on the phenomena of “telescopic memories.” These are vivid memories from different times that flood our psyche abruptly, bubbling up in our mind, and are experienced by us as standing in a doorway – as occurring right now or in the near future. They are one kind of “psychic zones.” That is, spaces of life which move and change over time both within our psyche and between our psyche and the other’s psyche. They have a reanimating function and a potential for constructing new meanings to our life in general and, even more so, specifically to the therapeutic relationship.*

responseSet.title: Telescopic Memories – “A Raid on the Inarticulate”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Telescopic Memories – “A Raid on the Inarticulate”

Rina Lazar PHD

Rina Lazar

Ramot Naftali 9, Tel Aviv 6927862, Israel
rinalazar61@gmail.com

Rina Lazar   Clinical psychologist in private practice in Tel-Aviv. She is a senior teacher and supervisor in the Core Program, the Relational Track and the Ph.D. Studies in Psychoanalysis and its Interfaces, the Program of Psychotherapy, Sackler Faculty of Medicine, Tel Aviv University. She is a member of the International Association of Relational Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy (IARPP) and the first chairperson of the Israeli chapter of the IARPP. Rina is the editor of Talking about Evil (Routledge, 2017), and the co-editor of Desire and The Blind Spot (Hakibutz Hameuchad, 2005, 2007).

Engaging with Beuchler’s response, this paper explores both the transformative impact of telescopic memories on the therapeutic relationship via the clinical material and the therapist’s function as the facilitator of such moments The paper emphasizes both the facilitating function of the therapist as well as the participatory one; the latter requires her to enter the storm to achieve the impact – the reverberation of interpsychic zones. Relating to moments of being (transformative moments), the author looks at the act of autotheoretical writing amid these horrific times of seemingly unending bloody conflict between Israelis and Palestinians as a way to preserve a residual sense of being while bearing the onslaught of tenacious moral injury.

responseSet.title: There and Then: Reflections on a Spreading Absence
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

There and Then: Reflections on a Spreading Absence

Maria Karakoshenko MA

Maria Karakoshenko

Lviv, Ukraine

Maria Karakoshenko  

I experience war as a spreading absence, an encroaching void. What was once reliable and effective is now sometimes unimaginable – it simply doesn’t come to mind. The voids – the gaps – are filled with desperate, disturbing, fruitless ruminations – efforts at least not to freeze in the face of what is approaching. The attempt to think breaks off, gets interrupted when it encounters what is no longer there, in the most unexpected places. And yet I continue, frantically trying to get back on my feet, like a May beetle on the pavement, in an effort to continue a unique, invaluable, coherent, unrepeatable path.

Sometimes I sit in front of a map of unfolding military operations and look at my first home there, separated from me by the front lines – here and now. And at the same time, it is still right inside me: in the language, in culinary and religious preferences. According to my internal chronograph, it was just yesterday – fully alive, filled with sounds and smells, breathing with a distinct atmosphere. And yet “here and now” already marks 10 years gone. And then I feel this creeping absence, like a film negative from which one could once develop and witness, revive frame by frame and fill with meaning – by discussing it with a close friend, or presenting it at conferences or in local museums – but now it’s stained with circular chemical burns. Opportunities are lost; only emptiness remains. On the negative, you can no longer distinguish That from This. You want to resist. To testify. To make meaning, to call things by their names. But sometimes you just sit, and all you can do is note that you’ve gone still.

Sometimes I sit in front of a client. Some fled from war. Some became it. Some had long ago become it and are only now recognizing that and finally synchronizing with the world. None of this is new – the scale of silence is. The repression of terror in the here and now, when anxiety fills the session, when rockets are flying. Or not flying. There are no guarantees; our reels capture less and less clarity. The film is losing its sensitivity to light; the apertures are more tightly closed. Sharpness is increasingly valued and prioritized over the creative, living frame with its soft light, imperceptibly imbued with a healing atmosphere from There and Then.

responseSet.title: Life Giving Denial: Surviving the Loss of Absolutes
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Life Giving Denial: Surviving the Loss of Absolutes

Nelia Narytsa MA

Nelia Narytsa

Vinnytsia, Ukraine

Nelia Narytsa  

I have been living and working in central Ukraine since the very beginning of the full-scale war. Outwardly, life now appears almost indistinguishable from pre-war times: home, work, family, sports, friends, education, rest. But that’s only on the surface.

The loss of primitive absolutes – the belief that tomorrow will come and resemble today – has changed life forever. Recovery from this realization came closer to summer. But as early as March 2, I began offering crisis support – though to whom more, myself or others? Soon after, patients began returning to resume therapy interrupted by the war. The calendar still holds the records of those events. Memory does not.

Back in March 2022, patients spoke about trying to place their experiences and the events occurring around them. Now, we almost never talk about the war. As if current matters – day-to-day life – are all I or my patients live by.

Themes of relationships with parents, partners, suffering from illnesses, and childhood events that influence adult life today – this is what my patients talk about. Only occasionally do some speak of the pain of children and families dying daily, of fear of mobilization, or anger toward our government.

Perhaps we all lack the words and meanings to speak of ourselves within the war. Perhaps denial shields us from the horrific parts of reality, where death is always close.

From here, I will speak only of myself, because these are my reflections and feelings. Perhaps, if I can say something about myself, I will find a way to hold the thoughts of my patients in our shared space, to offer them refuge and meaning, in the words of Ronald Britton.

However, Britton speaks of “refuge” in the Bionian sense of a container; I mean refuge as a meeting place of both interlocutors’ contexts. So, the question arises: what in the combination of my context and my patients prevents the creation of such a refuge for both of us in therapy? And how can we build it?

I lean here on the concept of unformulated traumatic experience. Both I and my patients are currently living through trauma, yet we speak of everything but the war. This phenomenon is widespread, as discussed at a conference that brought together colleagues from all corners of Ukraine.

responseSet.title: Infancy and Concern: A Response to Stephen Seligman
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Infancy and Concern: A Response to Stephen Seligman

Lesley Caldwell PHD

Lesley Caldwell

4 Lidfield Road, London N16 9NA, UK
l.caldwell@ucl.ac.uk

Lesley Caldwell   Retired member of the BPA and a Clinical Associate of the BPAS. She is currently the European representative on the Executive committee of the IPA. She is a Visiting Professor in the Psychoanalysis Unit at UCl where she taught on the Master’s program supervised doctoral students and organized the interdisciplinary program. She was director of the Squiggle Foundation from 2000 to 2003 a chair of the Winnicott Trust from 2008 to 2012. With Helen Taylor Robinson she is joint general editor of the Collected Works of Donald Winnicott (OUP, 2016). She has a long-standing interest in psychoanalysis and the arts, and clinically she has been particularly interested in analytic communication and the silent patient.

This response endorses Seligman’s approach to the baby’s experience of an initial state of helplessness and Winnicott’s overall focus on health and normal development, through his careful reading of concern as a developmental stage. The bodily presence of the mother/caregiver enables the change from a biological to a psycho-biological baby. What is required is provision not containment as the basis of both early infant care and later analytic care. It questions the contemporary focus on thinking by referring to earlier work by Martin James. Living and its meaning is based in the beginnings of recognition of the other that accompanies recognition of the self, its dependence on time and the centrality of the body in the acquisition of concern.

responseSet.title: A Discussion of Stephen Seligman’s Holding and Containing: “The Metaphor of the Baby” in Winnicott, Bion, and Klein
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Discussion of Stephen Seligman’s Holding and Containing: “The Metaphor of the Baby” in Winnicott, Bion, and Klein

Denis Flynn

Denis Flynn

London
57, Grove Road, Surbiton, Surrey KT6 4BY, UK
deniscflynn@yahoo.co.uk

Denis Flynn   Training and Supervising Analyst and a Child Analyst of the British Psychoanalytical Society/IPA working in private practice. Trained first as a Child Psychotherapist at the Tavistock Clinic he worked also for 25 years in the NHS (National Health Service) with children, mother/infant and family work, and was the for ten years Head of the Inpatient Adolescent Unit, treating severely borderline and suicidal adolescents, at the Cassel Hospital. He has taught widely here and abroad and held academic positions at University College London (Hon. Senior Lecturer) and at the University of Essex (Visiting Professor). He has worked in different areas of training and was until recently Head of Training Progression, then Chair of Education at the Institute of Psychoanalysis. He has published Severe Emotional Disturbance in Children and Adolescents: Psychotherapy in Applied Contexts, Brunner-Routledge (2004), The Internal and External Worlds of Children and Adolescents: Collaborative Therapeutic Care, Eds Day and Flynn (2003), other papers/chapters since then on adult, child and adolescent psychoanalysis, and has two short books on the early and later work of W.R. Bion in preparation for publication (in 2025 by Routledge).

Stephen Seligman gives an understanding of infants and the infantile based on Winnicott which is close to Bion’s in its emphasis on the relationship with the actual carer. However Bion’s and Klein’s emphasis on the ubiquity of frustration, with tension and early anxieties in early relationships underlying his theory of containment and underpinned by a “dual instinct” (or “drive”) theory – the conflict of life and death drives, he finds unsatisfactory. He concedes however it is a brilliant account of what happens when things go wrong, although he claims most infant development is not of this kind and is “good enough.” Denis Flynn’s discussion looks at Bion’s description of internal processing in a growing mind as a feature in the developing picture of any child even when there is no overt deprivation or trauma. He sees Bion’s containment like Winnicott’s original “use of an object” as describing the quality of emotional connection, and it is in no way “solipsistic” or “narcissistic.” He notes too how a wider understanding of Bion’s “theory of thinking” includes “positive realizations” not just “negative realizations,” and it is built upon steady emotional and symbolic growth in a progressive natural movement, similar to infant development as Stephen and Winnicott describe it.

responseSet.title: Holding and Containing: “The Metaphor of the Baby” in Winnicott, Bion, and Klein
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Holding and Containing: “The Metaphor of the Baby” in Winnicott, Bion, and Klein**

Stephen Seligman D.M.H.

Stephen Seligman

San Francisco, 21 Vicksburg St, San Francisco, CA 94114, USA
stephen.seligman@ucsf.edu

Stephen Seligman   Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences at the University of California, San Francisco, and NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis, and a Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis & Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California. His Relationships in Development: Infancy, Intersubjectivity, Attachment (Routledge, 2018) has been translated into Italian, Spanish, Chinese, Korean and Ukrainian (delayed). He also co-edited the Infant and Early Childhood Mental Health: Core Concepts and Clinical Practice (American Psychiatric Press) and is Editor-in-Chief Emeritus of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

Bion and Winnicott proposed distinctive images of infancy and the infantile, which reflected their more general theoretical and clinical tendencies, each evolving from Melanie Klein’s emphasis on object relations. Winnicott went further in prioritizing both object relations and the actual environment while rejecting the idea of the Death Instinct and the paranoid-schizoid positions: His view of the infant–mother relation emphasized interpenetrating, shared, and relatively comfortable presence. Although Bion introduced an important role for the mother in transforming potentially disorganizing “bad” projections, he stayed closer to Klein’s model in featuring frustration and its management as a primary source of normal development. The difference between Winnicott’s “holding” and Bion’s “containment” is clarified: Holding connotes a more mutual and harmonious arrangement, while containment implies that there is something potentially disruptive to contain. Clinical implications are noted.

[[RunningHead]]

Toward an Inclusive Spirit: Clarifying Harmonies and Differences – Response to Caldwell and Flynn Commentaries on “Bion’s and Winnicott’s Babies”

Stephen Seligman D.M.H.

Stephen Seligman

San Francisco, 21 Vicksburg St, San Francisco, CA 94114, USA
stephen.seligman@ucsf.edu

Stephen Seligman   Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences at the University of California, San Francisco, and NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis, and a Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis & Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California. His Relationships in Development: Infancy, Intersubjectivity, Attachment (Routledge, 2018) has been translated into Italian, Spanish, Chinese, Korean, and Ukrainian (delayed). He also co-edited of the Infant and Early Childhood Mental Health: Core Concepts and Clinical Practice (American Psychiatric Press) and is Editor-in-Chief Emeritus of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

Hoping to further both commentaries’ spirit of collegial, insightful dialogue, I sharpen some differences with Professor Flynn while noting points of agreement. I wonder about rhetorical strategies that may blur such differences so as to protect established ideas, rather than create more theoretical and clinical versatility or lead us to modify or discard problematic concepts. In addition, I agree with Denis that my reading of the primary narcissism concept in Klein overlooks her move to object relations, while maintaining that she still prioritizes the instinctual world over the environmental. I generally concur with Professor Caldwell’s emphasis on active relations with the environment in the vital, personal sense of living-in-the-world.

[[RunningHead]]

Talking in a Time of Violent Destruction: Introduction to a Dialogue Between Orna Guralnik and Christine Harb

Jessica Benjamin PHD

Jessica Benjamin

New York, New York, USA
jessbenja2@gmail.com

Jessica Benjamin   Author of The Bonds of Love (1988) and Beyond Doer and Done To: Recognition Theory, Intersubjectivity and the Third (2018). Her other books are Like Subjects, Love Objects (1995) and Shadow of the Other (1998). She is a supervisor and faculty member of the New York University Postdoctoral Psychology program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis and co-founder of the Stephen Mitchell Relational Studies Center.

In this dialogue between Orna Guralnik and Christine Harb, we find a different kind of mutual analysis than we may have known or seen before. One that enlightens us about what psychoanalytic process in contexts of mass trauma and political difference can aspire to when partners in dialogue commit to intense honesty and the determination to be known by the Other as well as knowing them.

responseSet.title: “Many People Would Throw a Tantrum at This Point”: An Israeli and a Palestinian Discuss 7 October, Gaza, and the Future
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Many People Would Throw a Tantrum at This Point”: An Israeli and a Palestinian Discuss 7 October, Gaza, and the Future

Orna Guralnik Psy.D. and Christine Harb D.O. M.PH.

Christine Harb

401 Broadway Suite 1700, New York, NY 10013, USA
orna@lucid-consulting.com

New York, New York, USA

Christine Harb   Physician and public health practitioner who specializes in gender affirming care, reproductive justice, social medicine, and global health.

Orna Guralnik and Christine Harb met on the docuseries Couples Therapy in 2022. During filming, they respectively realized that while they both live in New York, Orna, the program’s couple’s therapist, is Israeli, and Christine, who participated in the series with her partner, is Palestinian. They kept in touch after the show ended, and since October 7th, 2023, they have spent more than 30 hours, on video calls and in person, discussing their views and applying a psychoanalytic couple’s therapy framework to engage with difficult subjects without the conversation breaking down. The editors present their dialogue believing it a significant contribution to the psychoanalytic literature on trauma and reconciliation. The conversation between Guralnik and Harb was originally published in The Guardian and is presented in Psychoanalytic Dialogues with a new introduction by the authors and an accompanying introductory essay by Jessica Benjamin.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In This Issue, Psychoanalytic Dialogues 35-1

Stephen Hartman PHD , Lauren Levine PHD , Amy Schwartz Cooney PHD and Jack Foehl PHD

Jack Foehl

Jack Foehl  

We launch this thirty-fifth edition of Psychoanalytic Dialogues extremely proud to be presenting articles by Rachel Karliner and Elise Geltman, each a winner of the first Lewis Aron Prize for an original paper by an author new to psychoanalytic publishing. As you will soon see, this issue of Dialogues embraces the expansiveness of Lew’s vision and his enthusiasm for a comprehensive psychoanalytic practice, one that inquires into the artfulness of clinical work, the clarity earned by rigorous theoretical study, a steadfast engagement with the social, and the generative potential of collaborative institutional practice. It takes a lot of work and a lot of people to pull this off. And so, in addition to thanking Lew, all the founders of this journal who spirit us forward, and our Editorial Board who keep us afloat, we are grateful to the many first-time contributors who made awarding this prize a fine celebration of Dialogues’ mission and an inspiring nod to our bright future. We write this preface as the world seems ever more precarious and the value of open dialogue appears less and less pervasive. With that in mind, we carry on, as did Lew, holding hope dear and investing in the next generation of psychoanalytic writers and leaders with great conviction.

We first conceived of the Lewis Aron Prize to honor Lew’s legacy and deep impact on our field and to further his commitment to fostering new generations of psychoanalytic writers. The results were overwhelming. Our team of reviewers considered 26 entries, each of which merited distinction. Each reviewer brought keen insights to the jury’s conversations about how these papers charted an Aronesque landscape. To borrow from the title of Lew’s collected papers edited by Galit Atlas, the overwhelming bounty we received in submissions and the gathering of colleagues to discuss them represents the best of what happens, “When Minds Meet.”

While we had originally intended to award the prize to one author, the jury equally admired Karliner’s and Geltman’s very different papers. When read together—each a winner in its own right—the two articles represent the breadth of Dialogues’ mission, the scope of Lew Aron’s work, and the wealth of material that we aim to present in the pages of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

responseSet.authorCitation: Hartman, S., Levine, L., Cooney, A. & Foehl, J.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editors’ Introduction: The Lewis Aron Prize

Stephen Hartman PHD , Lauren Levine PHD , Amy Schwartz Cooney PHD and Jack Foehl PHD

Jack Foehl

Jack Foehl  

Please join us in congratulating Rachel Karliner and Elise Geltman, the two winners of Psychoanalytic Dialogues’ 2025 Lewis Aron Prize for an original paper by an author who brings a new voice to psychoanalytic journals.

The prize jury considered twenty-three papers from an international mix of authors, each of whom was inspired by some aspect of Aron’s scholarship and vision for a just and inclusive psychoanalysis. Reading these papers was an honor and a gift. The wealth of voices and topics gave us great confidence in the future of psychoanalytic writing.

While we had originally intended to award the prize to one author, the jury admired Karliner’s and Geltman’s very different papers equally. When read together, each a winner in its own right, the two articles represent the breadth of Dialogues’ mission, the scope of Lew Aron’s work, and the wealth of material that we aim to present in the pages of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

Rachel Karliner’s paper, Meeting in the In-Between: Grief, Immobility and Transformation, impressed the jury for its originality as well as its creativity and use of metaphor. Taking George Saunders’ Pulitzer Prize winning novel, Lincoln in the Bardo as her muse, Karliner elaborates a creative frame to represent liminal spaces in treatment and enter the mind of a grieving parent. In a beautifully narrated and deeply moving case that brought Karliner and the father of a recently deceased infant into a shared scene of grief, Karliner joins the man in his psychic Bardo where the therapeutic dyad is suspended between unbearable loss and the imperative to continue engaging meaningfully in life.

The jury applauded Geltman’s observations in Who is Free to Free Associate: Psychoanalysis and Social Ethics for its originality, grit, and integrity. Geltman’s examination of her training Institute as a “case” was called “insightful, generative, and nonjudgmental.” All the while, Geltman’s frank self-examination allowed her to elaborate how a “trickle down” theory of “psychic growth” influences our “institutional habitus” in a way that links theory to practice at multiple levels reminiscent of Aron’s later writings on the ethics of clinical practice.

responseSet.title: Meeting in the In-Between: Grief, Immobility, and Transformation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Meeting in the In-Between: Grief, Immobility, and Transformation

Rachel Karliner PHD

Rachel Karliner

New York, New York, USA
113 University Place, 11L, New York, NY 10003
rkarliner1@gmail.com

Rachel Karliner   Graduate of the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. She has published and presented on topics relating to the process of psychotherapy, including the function of curiosity, symbolic thinking, the significance of early memories, and the role of the analyst in play therapy with children. Her personal essay, “On Becoming the Tooth Fairy” was published in the collection “Writing on the Moon.” Dr. Karliner has supervised doctoral students at the National Institute for the Psychotherapies and the Gordon F. Derner School of Psychology. She is in private practice in New York City.

This paper explores the in-between state of grief, when a mourner is suspended between the crushing reality of loss, the disbelief of that very same loss, and the unthinkable demand to carry on with living. Using George Saunders’s depiction of the bardo – the intermediate dimension between this life and the next – I propose the bardo as a metaphor both for the liminal space of treatment and for the mind of the grieving patient. I describe meeting my patient in the in-between spaces of Zoom, as he enacted his unsettledness in mind and body several years after the death of his first-born child. Following him in his back-and-forth rhythm, I joined him in a suspended state, caught between facing the reality of death and engaging in the vitality of life. I suggest that joining him in this way was a kind of witnessing, which allowed his tragedy to enter and ultimately transform the field of treatment. Countertransference images of peaceful death and contagious trauma are explored, highlighting the need for the analyst to tolerate vulnerability in the face of tragedy.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Who is Free to Free Associate: Psychoanalysis and Social Ethics (AKA “Look a Psychoanalyst!”1)1

Elise Geltman LCSW

Elise Geltman

San Francisco, California, USA
5665 College Ave., Suite 240-A, Oakland, CA 94618
elisegeltman.lcsw@gmail.com

Elise Geltman   Psychoanalytic social worker in private practice in Oakland, CA. She engages in psychoanalysis, psychotherapy, clinical and organizational consultation, and teaching. She continues her work in community mental health at the Westcoast Children’s Clinic. Elise has presented and consulted within a wide range of settings, including Access Institute, SFCP, NYU Post Doc, CFS, UC Berkeley Health & Human Rights Center, and beyond. Elise studies group relations via GREX, A.K. Rice, and CSGSS and is committed to group life and engaging individuals, systems, and the social. Elise is the current President of PINC, on the Community Psychoanalysis Track - Steering Committee, and the Race Working Group.

As new ethical questions emerge within psychoanalysis, this paper creatively considers the question: What is ethical care at the group or social level? Utilizing the framework of social ethics, Institutionalized Psychoanalysis and the “social” are taken up in two ways that are distinct but imbricated. First, Institutional Psychoanalysis is considered as an entity, force, and participant in the larger social matrix and related historical, political, and economic discourses. This exploration highlights the potential impacts of institutionalization on psychoanalysis from multiple angles – including philosophical, clinical, personal, and social. Unminded links to neoliberalism are questioned, and the psychoanalytic goods of “internal freedom” and “psychic growth” are problematized. The author introduces the idea of “trickle-down psychic economies.” Second, the group life of Institutional Psychoanalysis is then taken up directly by exploring the unminded group that we make and repeatedly remake despite innovations and evolving commitments. Clinical material surfaces issues of whiteness, racial enactments, racialized reverie, unconscious group process, systemic and structural violence, and the necessity of “marking.” The author introduces the idea of “psychoanalytic habitus.” This paper is a passionate plea to Institutional Psychoanalysis (and those that comprise it) to take up a new kind of ethics and implication – together.

responseSet.title: Thinking Race: Exploring the Scene of Racial Enactment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thinking Race: Exploring the Scene of Racial Enactment

Bernadine H. Han MD

Bernadine H. Han

45 Plaza Street W, Ste 1A, Brooklyn, NY 11217, USA
beh9052@med.cornell.edu
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-0157-2214

Bernadine H. Han   Assistant clinical professor of psychiatry at Weill Cornell Medical College, and faculty and curriculum co-chair at the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research. She has a private practice in Brooklyn, NY, and is also on staff at South Beach Psychiatric Center’s outpatient clinics.

The scene of racial enactment animates ideas of the Other that are both implicitly internalized and unconsciously communicated. The receiver, themself subject to an uncontained experience, becomes the receptacle for both a dissociated process and disavowed content. This paper explores the visceral experience of received “microaggression” and an imagined group moment to study how intrapsychic and intra-group encounters with the fantasies and realities of racialized experience disrupt interpersonal processes of containment and recognition. I suggest that racial enactment involves the projection of both an unthought attack and unthoughtness itself, and these dissociations result in a confusing, illegitimized, and unthinkable experience in the receiver that simultaneously emanates from and results in a “psychological catastrophe”. A contagious breakdown of thinking triggers a threat of alienation and a search for substitutes to connection, such as a perversion of gratitude and the basic assumption of Oneness. Experiences of containment and creativity that bridge adamantly unthought realities of difference and their meanings to an explorable unknown can reorient the individual and the group toward the reestablishment of relationality, the restoration of thinking, and re-inhabitance of individual subjectivity.

Daniel G. Butler   Psychoanalytic candidate at PINC and a graduate of UC Santa Cruz’s History of Consciousness Program. He serves on the editorial boards of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, Studies in Gender and Sexuality, and JAPA: The Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association. His most recent publication, “Blackout,” appears in Psychoanalytic Dialogues. He teaches, supervises, and practices in San Francisco.

This discussion queries aspects of Jean Laplanche’s metapsychology and his distinction between psychotherapy and psychoanalysis. Reflecting on Georges Bataille’s analysis with Adrien Borel, I imagine psychoanalysis as an impossible convergence of the lytic and the therapeutic; and invoking the idea of anaclasis, which Laplanche rejects, I provocatively wonder if psychoanalysis leans on psychotherapy. Michel De M’Uzan’s work informs the intertwined hypotheses that I attempt to illustrate by revisiting one of Sam Guzzardi’s cases.

[[RunningHead]]

Fractal Seams and the Spaces in Between: Transpersonal Perspectives on the Liminal in Analytic Process

Yakov Shapiro MD and Deborah Bryon PHD

Deborah Bryon

2931 66 St NW, Edmonton, AB T6K 4C1, Canada
yshapiro@ualberta.ca
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-4885-6636

Denver, USA

Deborah Bryon   Member of the International Association of Jungian Analysts (IAAP) and the Inter-Regional Society of Jungian Analysts (IRSJA). She is in private practice in Madeira, Portugal, and the author of three books Lessons of the Inca shamans, Part I: Piercing the veil (Pine Winds Press, 2012) and Lessons of the Inca shamans, Part II: Beyond the veil (Pine Winds Press, 2014), and Time and Trauma in Analytical Psychology and Psychotherapy: The Wisdom of Andean shamanism Routledge, (2023). Recent publications include: “The Beauty of the Women in Willem de Kooning’s Paintings” in J of Analytical Psychology, 2024; “Implicit states of connectivity in the clinical practice of Jungian psychoanalysis and Andean shamanism” in J of Analytical Psychology, 2023; “Are Psychedelics Really Necessary?” in Psychedelics and Individuation, ed. L. Stein and L. Corbett (2023); “Bastard children of the avant-garde: artistic expression in the cubist and abstract expressionist movements and the psychoanalytic process” in J of Analytical Psychology, 2022; and “Processing trauma in psychoanalysis in real time and in dreams: The convergence of past, present, and future during Covid 19” in J of Analytic Psychology, 2021.

In this paper, we advocate a trans-theoretical approach to understanding the liminal space between conscious and unconscious processes, expanding the binary logic of Western psychology with the meta-reductive scientific perspectives and the indigenous Andean medicine tradition. A meta-theory description, inclusive of findings in quantum information, complexity, and fractal sciences combined with the ancestral Incan codes practiced by indigenous Andean medicine people (paqos) will be provided to map the uncharted dimensions of psychic spaces crossing temporal/atemporal boundaries in psychotherapy practice. Weaving together mind, brain, and psychophysical “uncanny” domains that happen within a clinical setting may further scientific and clinical understanding of the fractal patterning inherent within all living systems, and enhance the clinician’s capacity for therapeutic engagement in more profound and attuned ways. A systemic approach to formulating a deeper conceptualization of what constitutes the Self and boundaries between conscious and unconscious processes will be presented. It is the authors’ premise that psychological healing and emergence of new relational organization takes place in the fractal seams between conscious and unconscious self-states, where “uncanny” synchronistic connections bring new information and ways of being with each other into conscious awareness.

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 2025 35 1 35-45 Lewis Aron's Last Lectures: Jung and Contemporary Psychoanalysis Lewis Aron (1952–2019), was an internationally recognized psychoanalyst, sought-after teacher, lecturer, and the Director of the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. He was a past President of the Division of Psychoanalysis (39) APA; founding President of the International Association for Relational Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy (IARPP); founding President of the Division of Psychologist-Psychoanalysts of NYSPA. Aron was also the co-founder and co-chair of the Sándor Ferenczi Center at the New School for Social Research, and Adjunct Professor, School of Psychology, Interdisciplinary Center (IDC) Herzliya, Israel; A co-founder of Psychoanalytic Dialogues and the co-editor of the Relational Perspectives Book Series. He is the author and editor of numerous articles and books, including A Meetings of Minds, A Psychotherapy for the People (co-authored with Karen Starr) and, Dramatic Dialogue (co-authored with Galit Atlas). Zane Dodd Received his Doctorate in Counseling Psychology from the University of North Texas, where he completed specializations in both Marriage and Family, as well as Sport and Performance Psychology. He has worked in a variety of clinical settings over his career ranging from community clinics, counseling centers, psychiatric hospitals, and private practice. He co-founded the Chrysalis Center in 2019 and enjoys a wide range of clinical interests including complex trauma, personality difficulties, human performance, and their various intersections. He has served as the president of the Dallas Society of Psychoanalytic Psychology and the chair of the graduate student committee for Division 39 of APA. Galit Atlas Faculty member at NYU’s Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. She is the author of three books, including the international bestseller Emotional Inheritance, which has been translated into 27 languages. Atlas serves on the editorial boards of Psychoanalytic Dialogues and Psychoanalytic Perspectives. She is a recipient of the André François Award, the NADTA Research Award, the Gradiva Award, and the Nautilus Book Award. She is a psychoanalyst and clinical supervisor in private practice in New York City and teaches and lectures throughout the United States and internationally. Lewis Aron's Last Lectures: Jung and Contemporary Psychoanalysis

Lewis Aron PHD ABPP , Zane Dodd PHD and Galit Atlas PHD

Galit Atlas   Faculty member at NYU’s Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. She is the author of three books, including the international bestseller Emotional Inheritance, which has been translated into 27 languages. Atlas serves on the editorial boards of Psychoanalytic Dialogues and Psychoanalytic Perspectives. This paper presents selected excerpts from Lewis Aron’s lectures on Jung’s impact on psychoanalysis broadly and on relational psychoanalysis in particular. It explores the ways contemporary analysts, often without even knowing it, are building on Jung’s work. This series of lectures was given over the course of two meetings with a reading group in Dallas, Texas, on 9/11/2015 and 9/25/2015. Lew was known for his weekly private study groups. In addition to his in-person groups, in 2011, he began running virtual reading groups, and by the time of his death in February 2019, Lew had more than 150 weekly group members across the country and internationally. As anyone who attended one of Lew’s lectures could attest, he had a unique capacity to seamlessly weave historical context, the individual life experience of the theorists, and deep knowledge of theory into a narrative that felt much more like a story coming to life than a lecture. The effect was an incredibly engaging and richer understanding of both historical and current psychoanalytic theory and applications. It is our hope that by relaying some of these lectures, others can partake in some of the gifts we felt grateful to have experienced with him. This article is part of a forthcoming book featuring Aron’s last lectures.

Riccardo Lombardi MD

Riccardo Lombardi

Vicolo delle grotte 52, Roma 00186, Italy
dr.riccardolombardi54@gmail.com

Riccardo Lombardi   Psychiatrist and psychoanalyst, is full member of the International Psychoanalytic Association, training and supervising analyst of the Italian Psychoanalytic Society.

The transference onto the body is of essence with our current patients and particularly, when the analysand suffers from a body-mind dissociation. By this I mean she has an absence of emotional resonance, together with clear manifestations of estrangement from, or indifference toward himself and his own physicality. If it’s true that psychoanalysis is a relational process, it’s equally true that the focus of working through should be oriented toward the analysand herself – her way of relating to the concreteness of her own body, learning to understand her place and boundaries in space and time.

Understanding Diffraction as an Aspect of Working with Metaphor

Deborah Bryon PHD

Deborah Bryon

London, UK
Caminho da Fonte, nº 42, Parish of São Gonçalo, Madeira 9060-112, Portugal
deborahbryon@gmail.com

Deborah Bryon   Member of International Association of Jungian Analysts (IAAP) and Inter-Regional Society of Jungian Analysts (IRSJA). She is in private practice and the author of three books Lessons of the Inca shamans, Part I: Piercing the veil (Pine Winds Press, 2012), Lessons of the Inca shamans, Part II: Beyond the veil (Pine Winds Press, 2014), and Time and Trauma in Analytical Psychology and Psychotherapy: The Wisdom of Andean shamanism (Routledge, (2023) is in press. Her most recent publications include “Implicit states of connectivity in the clinical practice of Jungian psychoanalysis and Andean shamanism” (Journal of Analytical Psychology, 2023), “Are Psychedelics Really Necessary?” in Psychedelics and Individuation, ed Les Stein and Lionel Corbett, (2023), “Bastard children of the avant-garde: artistic expression in the cubist and abstract expressionist movements and the psychoanalytic process” (Journal of Analytical Psychology, May 2022), and “Processing trauma in psychoanalysis in real time and in dreams: The convergence of past, present, and future during covid 19” (Journal of Analytic Psychology, 2021).

Marks-Tarlow presents a compelling argument against reductive reasoning, while encouraging an interconnected non-linear perspective aligned with Einstein’s entanglement theory, the root of chaos, and complexity theories. Operating from the viewpoint that the universe has an underlying fractal organization, Marks-Tarlow has stated, “Fractal consciousness is the essential activity at the core of clinical intuition.” In my response, I use Marks-Tarlow’s conceptualization as a foundational point of departure, discussing the applicability of KarenBarad’s theory of diffraction in quantum theory toward deepening an understanding of the mechanics of what happens within the fractal seams arising in the psychoanalytic process, creating points of emergence for previously unformulated psychic material to enter consciousness.

responseSet.title: Pseudo-Self and the Hidden Order of Detachment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Pseudo-Self and the Hidden Order of Detachment

Peter Goldberg PHD

Peter Goldberg

San Francisco, California, USA
909 Ordway Street, Albany, 94706
petergoldberg@restio.org

Peter Goldberg   Personal and Supervising Analyst at the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California, is co-chair of Faculty at the San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis, and on the faculty of the Wright Institute in Berkeley. He is a co-author of Here I’m Alive: The Spirit of Music in Psychoanalysis (Columbia University Press, 2023) and has written and presented widely on a range of clinical and theoretical topics including the evolution of clinical theory in psychoanalysis; psychosomatic dissociation, sensory experience in analysis and cultural life; transitional mechanisms and the function of the analytic frame; non-representational states; and the impact of social trauma on individual psychology.

Gila Ashtor’s conception of the Pseudo-Self is explored in relation to her critique of three existing clinical approaches to dissociation. Important features of the pseudo-self are highlighted, including its distinctive blending in with current cultural formations, and its corresponding non-visibility in clinical theory, andd the reflexive type of detachment that place these patients beyond the usual reach of our usual clinical methods. The question of the clinical approach to dissociation, and to the phenomenon of the pseudo self in particular, is considered in light of Ashtor’s suggestion that unconscious defensive structures must be analyzed in order to treat dissociative disorders of feeling. Emphasis is placed on an active type of clinical engagement aimed at making contact with the pseudo-normal patient at the psycho-sensory level of shared experience. Let me say at the outset that it is impossible for me to do justice to the scope of Gila Ashtor’s incisive, comprehensive thought-provoking and paper. I must necessarily choose only a few points to focus on, leaving much untouched. Having spent such a rewarding time with the text, and feeling as I do an affinity with much of what is contained in it, I hope it will not be presumptuous if I refer to her as Gila in this discussion – to be on first name terms, as it were, although we have not met.
